(2) Subscription TAM: approximately $2B in annual recurring revenue at full penetration. Total annual market value at maturity: approximately $3.2B ($1.2B hardware + $2B subscription). Peloton's current revenue is approximately $717M (FY2024) — implying meaningful headroom if penetration continues to grow toward the $3.2B combined TAM ceiling.

keyTerms.definition: Exercise equipment integrated with a digital platform delivering live and on-demand workout classes — the hardware (bike, treadmill) and software subscription (Peloton content) are bundled into one product ecosystem
Annual Recurring Revenue from subscriptions — the most valuable part of Peloton's business model; approximately $504/year per subscriber, higher-margin and more predictable than hardware sales
The average number of years between a consumer purchasing a new connected fitness unit — estimated at 7 years; determines the annual hardware demand from an installed base at steady state

The percentage of the target household universe that adopts connected fitness; market research suggests 8-12% of households are likely buyers; the difference between 8% and 12% drives a $2-3B swing in TAM

The percentage of hardware buyers who also subscribe to the content subscription; estimated at 70% — meaning roughly 30% of equipment owners don't pay for the subscription (they use other content or no digital classes)

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer explicitly structures this as a two-part TAM — hardware (one-time) and subscription (recurring) — and proactively notes that for a PE firm, the subscription ARR is more strategically important. A weak answer treats this as a single market size without separating hardware from subscription economics, or fails to sanity-check against Peloton's reported revenue figures to validate the estimates.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I size this market, I want to flag something important for a PE context: connected fitness is a two-part market with very different economics. Hardware is one-time revenue with a long replacement cycle. Subscription is recurring, high-margin, and is the real value driver in a PE evaluation. I'll build both estimates separately using SPEAK — starting with a bottom-up household analysis, then sanity-checking against Peloton's reported financials to make sure my assumptions are grounded. Quick clarifying question: are we sizing the current 2024 market, or the potential market at realistic full penetration?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: I'll also ask: what does 'connected fitness equipment' include — are we limiting this to premium large-format equipment like Peloton bikes and treadmills at $1,200 to $2,000, or do we include smaller connected fitness products like Mirror-style screens or Apple Fitness+ which don't require proprietary hardware? I'll assume the premium large-format category only, since that's the Peloton competitive set, and I'll flag the broader market as an extension.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — State Assumptions
sampleResponse.script: My key assumptions. US total households: 134 million. Households with income $75,000 or above — the connected fitness buyer profile based on average price point of $1,500 for a bike: approximately 42% of households, or 56 million. Connected fitness purchase propensity: the case gives us 8 to 12% from market research — I'll use 10% as my working midpoint. Target household universe: 56 million times 10% equals 5.6 million households. Average equipment price: $1,500, the midpoint of the $1,200 to $2,000 range. Monthly subscription: $42, the midpoint of $39 to $44. I'll note explicitly that changing the propensity rate from 8% to 12% swings the subscriber count from 4.5 million to 6.7 million — that's the most sensitive assumption in this model.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Hardware TAM
sampleResponse.script: Hardware TAM: 5.6 million addressable households times $1,500 average equipment price equals $8.4 billion in total potential hardware opportunity. But this is one-time — a household only buys one connected bike. To annualize it, I assume a 7-year replacement cycle: $8.4 billion divided by 7 years equals $1.2 billion in annual hardware demand at steady-state full penetration. Today, with an installed base of roughly 4 million units and a 7-year cycle, annual replacement demand is approximately 570,000 units per year — about $855 million. That maps reasonably well to Peloton's current hardware revenue in the $400 to $500 million range, since Peloton has approximately 75% market share in the premium connected fitness category.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Subscription TAM
sampleResponse.script: Subscription TAM is the more important number for a PE analysis. Of the 5.6 million addressable equipment owners, I'll apply a 70% subscription attach rate — meaning 30% of owners either don't subscribe or use a competitor's content. That gives 3.9 million potential subscribers. At $42 per month, annual revenue per subscriber is $504. Total subscription TAM: 3.9 million times $504 equals approximately $1.97 billion in annual recurring revenue — call it $2 billion. Peloton currently reports roughly 2.97 million connected fitness subscribers generating approximately $1.5 billion in subscription revenue. Our model implies Peloton has about 75% of the subscriber market, which is consistent with their reported share. The sanity check holds.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — PE-Relevant Framing
sampleResponse.script: For a PE evaluation, here's how I'd frame the two-part market. The hardware TAM at steady-state is $1.2 billion annually — mature, cyclical, and dependent on new household formation and replacement demand. The subscription TAM is $2 billion annually at full penetration, growing as new users onboard. The subscription business is 60 to 70% gross margin; hardware is 15 to 25%. In a PE context, you're essentially buying the subscription flywheel, not the hardware business. The question is whether the installed base of 4 million units is enough to sustain $1.5 billion in subscription revenue and grow it — or whether subscriber churn and competitive pressures from Apple Fitness+, Hydrow, and NordicTrack erode the base.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: the total addressable market for US connected fitness has two parts. The hardware TAM is $8.4 billion in total potential units, or approximately $1.2 billion annually at replacement-cycle steady-state. The subscription TAM is approximately $2 billion in annual recurring revenue at realistic full penetration of 10% of income-qualified households. Combined annual market value at maturity: approximately $3.2 billion. Peloton's current revenue of approximately $717 million (FY2024) implies the company is at approximately 22% of the combined TAM — suggesting meaningful headroom if penetration continues to grow, but also significant execution risk given the post-COVID subscriber plateau. For the PE evaluation, I'd focus the diligence on subscriber churn rate and the sustainability of the subscription attach rate — those two metrics determine whether the $2 billion subscription TAM is a ceiling Peloton can approach or one it's already begun retreating from.

title: Sizing the US Prescription Eyeglass Market
prompt: Warby Parker wants to size the US prescription eyeglass market to assess expansion opportunity. The CEO needs a credible estimate to present to investors. How would you structure this, and what is your estimate of the total market size?
context: Approximately 75% of US adults need vision correction (glasses or contacts). US adult population is approximately 258M. Of those needing correction, about 64% primarily use glasses and 36% use contact lenses. Americans replace their glasses every 2.4 years on average. Average out-of-pocket spend per pair is $260 (vs. Warby Parker's $95-$200 range). Luxottica/EssilorLuxottica dominates the optical retail market with $10B+ in US revenue.
whyThisFramework: SPEAK guides the estimate: State that 75% of 258M adults need correction; of those, 64% use glasses (124M people); glasses are replaced every 2.4 years, so annual 'replacement demand' = 124M ÷ 2.4 = 52M pairs/year; at $260 average ASP = $13.5B TAM. Cross-check against reported $16B US optical market to validate order of magnitude.
keyConsiderations: 75% of 258M adults = 194M needing correction; 64% using glasses = 124M glasses wearers
Annual replacement: 124M ÷ 2.4 years = 52M pairs per year
Revenue: 52M pairs × $260 average = $13.5B annual market
Warby Parker at $95-200 ASP serves a mid-market segment — their realistic SAM is about $4-5B
clarifyingQuestions: Are we sizing total retail value (consumer out-of-pocket) or wholesale/manufacturer revenue — and should insurance reimbursements be included or excluded?
Should we include premium frames-only purchases by people who already have a current prescription, or only new prescription-driven purchases?
Are we sizing the full US adult market or just Warby Parker's serviceable addressable market given their $95-$200 price range?
Should we account for first-time glasses wearers entering the market, or model this as a pure replacement cycle?

subIssues: How many US adults need vision correction of any kind?
Of those, what share primarily use glasses vs. contact lenses?
Are there demographic segments (age, income) that skew toward glasses vs. contacts?
What is the average replacement cycle for prescription glasses?
How many first-time buyers enter the market each year (e.g., new prescriptions for aging adults)?
Do multi-pair buyers materially change the unit volume calculation?
What is the average out-of-pocket spend per pair across all retail channels?
How does ASP differ across price segments (budget, mid-market like Warby, luxury)?
What portion of revenue is frames vs. lenses vs. coatings?

stepByStep.title: Anchor the addressable population
stepByStep.detail: Start with the 258M US adults, apply 75% needing correction = 194M, then the 64% glasses split = 124M active glasses wearers. State these numbers explicitly and ask if the interviewer wants you to refine any assumption.

stepByStep.title: Calculate annual unit demand
stepByStep.detail: Divide 124M wearers by the 2.4-year replacement cycle to get ~52M pairs purchased annually. Note that first-time buyers add a small increment (~2-3M/year from aging population) but are secondary to replacement demand.

stepByStep.title: Apply average selling price
stepByStep.detail: Multiply 52M pairs × $260 average out-of-pocket ASP to reach ~$13.5B TAM. Flag that this is consumer-paid retail value; wholesale revenue would be 40-50% lower.

stepByStep.title: Sanity check against known data
stepByStep.detail: Cross-check: Luxottica's US revenue is $10B+; the US optical retail market is widely cited at $16B. Your $13.5B estimate is directionally consistent — it likely understates because it excludes vision insurance flows and premium/luxury spend.

stepByStep.title: Identify Warby Parker's SAM
stepByStep.detail: Warby Parker targets mid-market at $95-$200 vs. $260 average. Estimate ~35% of the market falls in this price tier = ~$4.7B SAM. This is the number most relevant to the investor presentation.

keyTerms.definition: Total Addressable Market — the full revenue opportunity if every potential buyer were captured; here, the entire US prescription eyeglass market (~$13.5B)
Serviceable Addressable Market — the subset of TAM reachable given a company's price point, geography, and distribution; Warby Parker's SAM is ~$4-5B
Average Selling Price — the average revenue per unit transaction; the US eyeglass ASP is ~$260 out-of-pocket per pair
How often customers repurchase — glasses average 2.4 years, which drives annual unit volume even without population growth
The share of the population requiring corrective lenses; approximately 75% of US adults, split 64% glasses / 36% contacts

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer segments the population cleanly (needs correction → glasses vs. contacts → annual pairs), applies the replacement cycle correctly, and cross-checks the output against the known $16B optical market. A weak answer skips the glasses/contacts split, fails to use the replacement cycle, or cannot articulate what portion of the market Warby Parker can actually serve.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Great question. Before I dive in, I want to confirm one assumption: we're sizing the consumer retail market in terms of out-of-pocket spend, and we're focused on prescription glasses rather than the full optical market including contacts and sunglasses. I'll use a bottom-up demand-side build — start with the population that needs glasses, model how often they buy, and multiply by the average price. Then I'll cross-check against known market data. Does that sound right?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Two quick questions before I build the math. First — should I include vision insurance reimbursements in the revenue estimate, or just out-of-pocket consumer spend? The prompt mentions $260 average out-of-pocket, so I'll use that. Second — are we sizing Warby Parker's specific opportunity, or the full US prescription glasses market? I'll size the full market first, then flag Warby's serviceable segment.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Population Segmentation
sampleResponse.script: Starting with the US adult population: 258 million adults. We know about 75% need some form of vision correction — that's roughly 194 million people. Of those, 64% primarily use glasses versus 36% who use contacts. So our glasses-wearing population is 194 million times 64%, which is approximately 124 million people actively wearing prescription glasses.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Annual Purchase Volume
sampleResponse.script: Now, how many pairs are bought each year? Americans replace glasses every 2.4 years on average. So I take 124 million wearers divided by 2.4 years — that gives me roughly 52 million pairs purchased annually. I'll add a small increment for first-time buyers, maybe 2 to 3 million from the aging population entering the glasses market each year, but replacement demand dominates, so I'll round to about 52 to 54 million pairs per year.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Revenue Calculation
sampleResponse.script: At $260 average out-of-pocket spend per pair, I get 52 million pairs times $260, which equals approximately $13.5 billion in annual consumer spend. That's my TAM estimate for the US prescription eyeglass market.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Sanity Check
sampleResponse.script: Let me validate that. EssilorLuxottica — the dominant player — reports over $10 billion in US revenue. Industry sources cite the total US optical retail market at around $16 billion, which includes contacts and premium luxury frames. My $13.5 billion for prescription glasses alone is directionally consistent — slightly conservative because it excludes vision insurance flows and luxury segment spend above $260. I'm comfortable the estimate is in the right order of magnitude.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: the US prescription eyeglass market is approximately $13.5 billion in annual consumer spend. Warby Parker, priced at $95 to $200 versus the $260 average, targets the mid-market tier. If roughly 35% of purchases fall in that price band, Warby's serviceable addressable market is approximately $4.7 billion. That's the number most relevant for the investor presentation — it shows meaningful scale while honestly reflecting where Warby competes rather than overclaiming the full $13.5 billion.

title: Sizing the US Rideshare Market
prompt: A venture capital firm is evaluating a Lyft competitor focused on the suburbs. Before pitching, they need a defensible estimate of the total US rideshare market size. How would you build the estimate, and what is your conclusion?
context: US population is 335M. The adult population (18+) is approximately 258M. Rideshare usage requires smartphone ownership (approximately 85% of US adults). Urban and suburban rideshare usage differs significantly — urban users average 4.2 rides per month, suburban users 1.1 rides per month. Average rideshare transaction is $18-22. Major urban areas (top 50 cities) account for approximately 72% of rideshare volume.
whyThisFramework: SPEAK drives segmented estimation: urban users (top 50 cities = roughly 40% of adult population × 85% smartphone penetration × 30% rideshare users at high frequency) vs. suburban/rural (60% of adults × 85% smartphone × 12% rideshare usage at lower frequency). Sum ride counts, multiply by average fare, sanity-check against reported $28-32B Uber + Lyft combined US GMV. Note: the 40% urban population share and 72% urban rideshare volume share are consistent — urban riders take rides at dramatically higher frequency, so a minority of the population generates a large majority of volume.
keyConsiderations: Urban adult rideshare users: 258M × 40% urban × 85% smartphone × 30% rideshare = ~26M users
Suburban/rural: 258M × 60% × 85% × 12% = ~16M occasional users
Urban: 26M × 4.2 rides/month × $20 × 12 = $26.2B GMV; Suburban: 16M × 1.1 × $20 × 12 = $4.2B
Total ~$30B GMV gross market; net revenue to Lyft/Uber after driver pay is approximately 25-28% = $7.5-8.4B
clarifyingQuestions: Are we sizing gross merchandise value (total fares paid) or net platform revenue after driver payouts — this changes the answer by roughly 75%?
Should we include airport rides, corporate accounts, and food delivery as part of the rideshare market, or strictly peer-to-peer rides?
Is this a current-state market size or a 3-5 year projection, given the suburban competitor is still a startup?
Are we including all US adult rideshare users or limiting to the suburban segment relevant to this competitor's pitch?

subIssues: How many adults live in major US urban markets (top 50 cities)?
What share of urban adults use rideshare and at what frequency?
What is the average urban fare and how does it differ by city tier?
How many adults live in suburban and rural markets?
What share use rideshare in these markets and how often?
What are the characteristics of suburban rides — longer distance, lower frequency?
What is the gross fare per ride across both segments?
What is the platform take rate that converts GMV to net revenue?
How does driver supply constraint affect market size in suburban areas?

stepByStep.title: Segment the addressable adult population
stepByStep.detail: Split 258M adults into urban (40% = 103M) and suburban/rural (60% = 155M). Apply 85% smartphone penetration to each segment to establish the rideshare-eligible base. Note: the context states 72% of rideshare volume comes from urban areas — this is a volume share, not a population share. Urban riders take rides far more frequently, which is why 40% of the population generates 72% of total rides.

stepByStep.title: Apply rideshare adoption rates by segment
stepByStep.detail: Urban: 103M × 85% smartphone × 30% active rideshare users = ~26M urban rideshare users. Suburban/rural: 155M × 85% × 12% = ~16M occasional suburban users. Justify the 30% vs. 12% differential with the car-ownership and public transit context.

stepByStep.title: Model annual ride volume by segment
stepByStep.detail: Urban: 26M users × 4.2 rides/month × 12 months = ~1.31B rides. Suburban: 16M users × 1.1 rides/month × 12 = ~211M rides. Total: approximately 1.52 billion rides annually. Quick sanity check: urban rides (1.31B) ÷ total rides (1.52B) = 86% — this is directionally consistent with the context's 72% urban volume share (some gap is expected because our adoption and frequency estimates are approximate).

stepByStep.title: Calculate GMV and net revenue
stepByStep.detail: At $20 average fare: 1.52B rides × $20 = $30.4B GMV. Platform take rate of 25-28% yields $7.6-8.5B net revenue to Uber/Lyft combined. State which metric is most relevant to the VC pitch (GMV for market scale, net revenue for unit economics).

stepByStep.title: Sanity check and suburban opportunity flag
stepByStep.detail: Cross-check: Uber and Lyft report combined US GMV around $28-32B — consistent. For the suburban competitor, the 211M suburban rides × $20 = ~$4.2B GMV is the addressable opportunity, with take-rate revenue of approximately $1B — a defensible venture-scale opportunity.

keyTerms.definition: Gross Merchandise Value — the total dollar value of all transactions on a platform before subtracting driver payouts; the rideshare market's ~$30B figure is GMV, not platform revenue
The percentage of GMV retained as platform revenue after paying drivers; Uber and Lyft typically take 25-28%, meaning net revenue is roughly one-quarter of gross fares
The share of smartphone-owning adults who actively use rideshare — approximately 30% in urban markets and 12% in suburban markets due to car-ownership differences
The specific market the Lyft competitor targets: ~$4.2B GMV from suburban riders averaging 1.1 rides per month, largely underserved by current platforms
In suburban markets, the scarcity of drivers limits both ride frequency and rider adoption — a structural limit on market size that pure demand modeling would overstate

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer segments urban vs. suburban with distinct adoption rates and ride frequencies, correctly distinguishes GMV from net platform revenue, and closes with a crisp suburb-specific number relevant to the VC's question. A strong answer also addresses the apparent tension between 40% urban population share and 72% urban volume share — explaining that high urban ride frequency (4.2/month vs. 1.1/month) accounts for the gap. A weak answer applies a single national average rideshare rate to all adults and fails to connect the analysis to the suburban competitor's opportunity.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: To size the US rideshare market, I'm going to use a segmented bottom-up approach — separating urban and suburban rideshare behavior because the data shows they differ dramatically in frequency. I'll also want to be clear upfront whether we're discussing gross fares or net platform revenue, since those numbers differ by about 75%. My working assumption is we're sizing total GMV for market scale purposes, then I'll convert to net revenue. Shall I proceed?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Two quick clarifications: First, are we including ride-hailing only, or also the food delivery and freight segments that Uber operates? I'll assume ride-hailing only to keep this focused. Second, is this current-state or a 3-year projection? I'll size the current market, but I can talk through the suburban growth curve afterward.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Population Segmentation
sampleResponse.script: Starting with 258 million US adults. Rideshare requires a smartphone, and about 85% of US adults own one — so the eligible base is roughly 219 million. I'll split this into urban, which I'll define as residents of the top 50 metro areas, representing about 40% of adults — so 103 million urban adults. The remaining 60%, about 155 million, are suburban or rural. One thing I want to flag upfront: the context tells us that 72% of rideshare volume comes from urban areas. That might seem inconsistent with 40% of the population being urban — but it's not. Urban riders take rides far more often, 4.2 times a month versus 1.1 in the suburbs. A smaller population generating a much larger share of volume is exactly what we'd expect given that frequency difference.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Adoption Rates by Segment
sampleResponse.script: Of urban smartphone-owning adults, I estimate about 30% are active rideshare users — that's roughly 26 million people. In urban markets, you have limited parking, easy driver supply, and lower car-ownership rates, so 30% feels defensible. In suburban markets, car ownership is much higher and driver supply is thin, so I'll use 12% — giving us about 16 million suburban rideshare users.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Annual Ride Volume
sampleResponse.script: Urban riders average 4.2 rides per month from the data given, so: 26 million users times 4.2 rides times 12 months equals roughly 1.31 billion rides annually. Suburban users average 1.1 rides per month: 16 million times 1.1 times 12 equals about 211 million rides. Total rides in the US: approximately 1.52 billion per year. As a check: urban rides represent about 86% of my total, which is directionally consistent with the 72% urban volume share in the context — the difference reflects the approximations in my adoption and frequency assumptions.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Revenue Calculation
sampleResponse.script: At an average fare of $20, 1.52 billion rides gives us $30.4 billion in gross merchandise value. At a 25 to 28 percent platform take rate, net revenue to Uber and Lyft combined is approximately $7.6 to $8.5 billion. The $30 billion GMV number is what you'd use to describe market scale in a pitch deck; the $8 billion is what matters for unit economics discussions.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: the total US rideshare market is approximately $30 billion in GMV, or about $8 billion in net platform revenue. The cross-check holds — Uber and Lyft's combined reported US GMV is in the $28 to $32 billion range. For the suburban competitor specifically: 211 million suburban rides at $20 average is a $4.2 billion GMV opportunity, translating to roughly $1 billion in net revenue at scale. That's a legitimate venture-scale market, particularly if driver supply challenges can be solved, which is the real moat question for this startup.

title: Sizing the Premium Canned Water Market
prompt: Liquid Death is pitching to expand into the UK market and needs to size the premium water opportunity there. UK population is 67M. Estimate the UK market for premium canned/bottled water (defined as water priced above £1.50/unit) and how large a share Liquid Death could realistically capture in three years.
context: UK adults drink approximately 9.2L of water per week on average, with approximately 30% of that from packaged sources. UK bottled water market is approximately £2.8B. Premium water (Evian, Volvic, Highland Spring) holds 38% of the bottled water market. Canned beverages in the UK represent only 22% of the premium water format mix (vs. 60% in the US). Sustainability resonates strongly in the UK — 71% of UK consumers actively avoid single-use plastic.
whyThisFramework: SPEAK: State that the UK bottled water market is £2.8B; apply 38% premium penetration = £1.06B premium water market; canned format at 22% = £234M current canned premium water market; project TAM expansion as canned format grows with sustainability trend (37% of a slightly grown £1.15B premium market = ~£425M in 3 years); estimate Liquid Death's 3-year share at 5-8% of £425M = £21-34M, with 6% base case yielding ~£25M.
keyConsiderations: Premium water TAM: £2.8B × 38% premium = £1.06B; canned subset = 22% × £1.06B = £234M
Sustainability tailwind: UK's plastic ban trajectory could shift canned format to 35-40% in 3 years
Liquid Death's positioning ('murder your thirst,' punk aesthetic) may need UK cultural calibration
Realistic 3-year market share: 5-8% of £425M projected canned premium TAM = £21-34M revenue target; base case at 6% = ~£25M
clarifyingQuestions: Are we sizing the current canned premium water market or a projected 3-year TAM that accounts for format shift from plastic to cans — since the sustainability trend is a key driver?
Is Liquid Death's UK pricing expected to match their US price point (~$2-3 per can), which translates to £1.70-2.50 — well above the £1.50 premium threshold?
Should market share be estimated for Liquid Death as a standalone brand, or are we including a potential partnership with an existing UK distributor?
Are we measuring retail volume (cans sold) or retail value (£ revenue), and should we include DTC channels like Amazon UK?

subIssues: What is the total UK bottled water market in £?
What share qualifies as premium (above £1.50/unit)?
Of premium water, what share is currently in canned vs. plastic/glass format?
How fast is the UK shifting from plastic bottles to canned beverages?
What regulatory or sustainability trends accelerate canned water adoption?
What will the canned format share of premium water be in 3 years?
What share of the projected canned premium market can a new entrant realistically capture in 3 years?
How does Liquid Death's brand positioning translate to the UK consumer?
What distribution channels and investment level are assumed?

stepByStep.title: Establish the current premium water TAM
stepByStep.detail: Start with the given £2.8B UK bottled water market. Apply 38% premium penetration to get £1.06B in premium water. This is the total opportunity for brands like Liquid Death, Evian, and Highland Spring.

stepByStep.title: Isolate the canned format subset
stepByStep.detail: Canned beverages represent only 22% of the premium water format mix in the UK today. Apply 22% to £1.06B = £234M current canned premium water market. This is the beachhead opportunity, but it understates the 3-year opportunity given format shift.

stepByStep.title: Project 3-year format shift
stepByStep.detail: UK sustainability pressure (71% plastic avoidance, potential plastic bans) could push canned format share from 22% to 35-40% over 3 years. Apply 37% (midpoint) to a slightly grown £1.06B premium market (~£1.15B at 3% category growth) = £425M projected 3-year canned premium TAM.

stepByStep.title: Estimate realistic Liquid Death market share
stepByStep.detail: A new entrant in a developed beverage market typically achieves 5-8% share within 3 years with strong distribution and brand investment. Apply 6% to £425M = ~£25M. Cross-check: this implies roughly 12-14 million cans sold at £1.80 average — a plausible retail scale for a year-3 challenger brand.

stepByStep.title: Flag cultural and operational risks
stepByStep.detail: Raise two risks the interviewer will expect: (1) Liquid Death's punk/irreverent US aesthetic may require UK brand calibration — British consumers respond differently to American 'edgy' marketing; (2) UK retail distribution requires major grocery partnerships (Tesco, Sainsbury's) which take 12-18 months to establish.

keyTerms.definition: Packaged water priced above £1.50/unit, encompassing brands like Evian, Volvic, and Highland Spring; currently 38% of the UK bottled water market by value
The share of a product category sold in each packaging type (can, plastic bottle, glass); canned water is only 22% of UK premium water vs. 60% in the US — indicating significant upside
Growth in the total addressable market driven by structural trends rather than just category growth — here, the shift from plastic to cans driven by sustainability regulation
Direct-to-Consumer — selling via brand website or Amazon, bypassing traditional retail; critical for Liquid Death's early UK entry before securing major grocery listings
The share of a target market's purchases captured by a new entrant; 5-8% in 3 years is typical for a well-funded challenger brand in an established beverage category

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer layers the sizing correctly — current TAM, then format-shift projection, then realistic share — and proactively addresses brand localization risk. A weak answer takes the current £234M canned market at face value without projecting the sustainability-driven format shift, or claims an unrealistically high share for a new entrant.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: To size this opportunity, I'm going to do a two-stage build: first, the current UK premium canned water market; second, a 3-year projected TAM that accounts for the format shift from plastic to cans, which the sustainability data suggests is accelerating. Then I'll estimate Liquid Death's realistic share capture. My numbers will be in pounds, retail value basis. Sound good?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Before I start: two questions. First, is Liquid Death's UK pricing expected to stay consistent with their US positioning — roughly £1.80 to £2.50 per can — which puts them firmly in the premium tier above the £1.50 threshold? I'll assume yes. Second, should I assume Liquid Death enters through traditional retail, DTC, or both? I'll model retail-led with DTC as secondary, since that's the highest-impact route for reaching scale in year three.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Current Premium Water TAM
sampleResponse.script: Starting with the given data: the UK bottled water market is £2.8 billion. Premium water — brands above £1.50 per unit — holds 38% of that market, which gives us £1.06 billion as the current premium water TAM. That's the full opportunity for any premium water brand in the UK today.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Canned Format Subset
sampleResponse.script: Now, Liquid Death is specifically a canned water brand. In the UK, cans represent only 22% of the premium water format mix — much lower than the 60% in the US where Liquid Death operates. Applying 22% to £1.06 billion gives us £234 million as the current canned premium water market. That's the immediate beachhead, but I think it significantly understates the 3-year opportunity.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — 3-Year TAM Projection
sampleResponse.script: Here's why: 71% of UK consumers actively avoid single-use plastic, and the UK government has been progressing toward extended plastic packaging restrictions. I'd project the canned format share of premium water growing from 22% today to roughly 35-37% within three years — a conservative estimate given the regulatory and consumer trends. Applying 37% to a slightly grown premium market — call it £1.15 billion at 3% annual category growth — gives us approximately £425 million as the 3-year canned premium water TAM. That's nearly double the current market.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Liquid Death's Market Share
sampleResponse.script: For a new challenger brand entering a developed beverage market with strong distribution investment, 5 to 8 percent share in three years is realistic based on analogous US launches. I'll use 6 percent as my base case. Six percent of £425 million is approximately £25 million in retail revenue by year three. Cross-checking that: at £1.80 average retail price, that's about 14 million cans sold annually — the kind of volume a brand can reach with listings in Tesco and Sainsbury's plus meaningful DTC and online presence.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: the current UK canned premium water market is £234 million, but the 3-year TAM — accounting for the shift away from plastic — is approximately £425 million. Liquid Death's realistic year-three revenue target is approximately £25 million, assuming 6% market share. Two risks I'd flag for the pitch: first, the punk aesthetic may need some cultural calibration for the UK market — British consumers are skeptical of American irony-as-branding. Second, major UK grocery listings take 12 to 18 months to secure, so the distribution timeline needs to be built into the financial model. The opportunity is real and strategically sound; execution timing is the key variable.

title: Sizing the US Alcohol Delivery Market
prompt: Instacart is evaluating launching an alcohol delivery service in partnership with Total Wine and local liquor stores. Before investing in licensing and infrastructure, they need to size the total addressable market for on-demand alcohol delivery in the US. Build the estimate.
context: The US alcohol market is approximately $130B in total (retail + on-premise). Retail alcohol (off-premise) is approximately $70B. Current alcohol delivery penetration is approximately 4% of retail alcohol, concentrated in major metropolitan areas. Legal frameworks vary by state — 38 states allow alcohol delivery. Alcohol delivery orders average $65-85 per transaction. Drizly (acquired by Uber) was the market leader before being shut down in 2024.
whyThisFramework: SPEAK: State retail alcohol TAM is $70B; apply the 38-state legal availability filter (approximately 82% of US adult population); apply current 4% delivery penetration as baseline and project potential to 12-15% at maturity based on grocery delivery analogy. Calculate addressable revenue at multiple penetration scenarios and sanity-check against Drizly's reported $750M peak GMV.
keyConsiderations: Legal TAM: $70B retail × 82% population in legal states = $57.4B legal opportunity
Current penetration: $57.4B × 4% = $2.3B current market; mature potential at 12% = $6.9B
Instacart's existing grocery infrastructure reduces incremental investment cost significantly
Key risks: licensing complexity by state, liability insurance, age verification technology required
clarifyingQuestions: Are we sizing the current alcohol delivery market or the potential market at delivery penetration parity with grocery (approximately 12-15%)?
Is Instacart's fee revenue (take rate) the relevant metric, or gross merchandise value of alcohol delivered?
Should we include the on-premise market ($60B in bars/restaurants) as out of scope, or are there delivery-adjacent use cases like restaurant alcohol delivery?
Are we limited to the 38 states that currently allow alcohol delivery, or should we project a regulatory pathway to more states?

subIssues: What is the total US retail alcohol market in dollar value?
What share of the US adult population lives in the 38 states that permit alcohol delivery?
Are there meaningful city or county-level restrictions within legal states that further narrow the TAM?
What is the current delivery penetration rate for alcohol?
What penetration rate is reasonable at market maturity based on grocery delivery analogy?
What are the barriers to penetration growth — cost, convenience, regulatory, consumer behavior?
What is Instacart's take rate on alcohol delivery orders vs. grocery?
How does average order value for alcohol ($65-85) compare to Instacart's grocery AOV, and how does that affect fee economics?
What incremental infrastructure and licensing costs must be netted against gross revenue?

stepByStep.title: Establish the legal TAM
stepByStep.detail: Start with $70B retail alcohol market. Apply the 38-state legal filter: these states contain approximately 82% of the US adult population, giving a legal TAM of $70B × 82% = $57.4B. This is the outer bound of the addressable market.

stepByStep.title: Apply current delivery penetration
stepByStep.detail: At 4% current delivery penetration, the current alcohol delivery market is $57.4B × 4% = ~$2.3B GMV. Cross-check: Drizly at peak was the leader with ~$750M GMV; add Gopuff, Minibar, and others and ~$2-2.5B current market feels right.

stepByStep.title: Project maturity penetration
stepByStep.detail: Grocery delivery reached ~12-15% penetration at maturity (pre-COVID baseline, post-COVID normalization). Alcohol delivery faces additional regulatory friction, so a conservative 10-12% maturity penetration is reasonable. At 12%: $57.4B × 12% = $6.9B mature GMV.

stepByStep.title: Calculate Instacart's revenue opportunity
stepByStep.detail: Instacart's take rate on grocery is typically 10-15% including delivery fees and service fees. Alcohol may carry a slight premium for liability reasons — call it 12-15%. Apply 13% to the $6.9B mature GMV = ~$897M annual revenue opportunity for Instacart at full maturity.

stepByStep.title: Flag key risks
stepByStep.detail: Three investor-level risks: (1) State-by-state licensing is expensive and slow; (2) Age verification requirements create liability exposure; (3) With Drizly shut down, Instacart must educate the market again, which extends the adoption curve.

keyTerms.definition: Alcohol sold through retail channels (liquor stores, grocery, convenience) for consumption off-site — the $70B market relevant to delivery; excludes $60B in on-premise bar and restaurant sales
The share of a retail category's total sales that occur via on-demand delivery; alcohol is currently at 4% penetration vs. grocery's 12-15% at maturity
Gross Merchandise Value — total retail value of alcohol delivered; Instacart's actual revenue is ~13% of GMV via take rate
Platform fee as a percentage of GMV; Instacart charges approximately 10-15% on grocery orders, likely 12-15% on alcohol given liability considerations
Digital or in-person ID verification required to legally complete alcohol delivery; a compliance cost and consumer friction point that depresses penetration relative to grocery delivery

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer correctly applies the legal state filter before the penetration rate, builds both a current-state and mature-market scenario, and translates GMV to Instacart's actual revenue. A weak answer applies national penetration data without the legal constraint, or conflates GMV with Instacart's take-rate revenue.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: I'm going to size this in two scenarios: the current alcohol delivery market, and the mature-state opportunity that Instacart would be building toward. I'll use a top-down approach — starting from total retail alcohol, applying the legal availability filter, then penetration rates. I'll also separate GMV from Instacart's actual revenue since those are very different numbers. Is that the right framing?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Two quick questions. First — should I focus on GMV, or does leadership want to see Instacart's take-rate revenue? I'll build both. Second — are we assuming alcohol delivery is offered as an add-on to existing grocery orders, or as a standalone service? I'll model it as standalone to be conservative, since basket dynamics differ.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Legal TAM
sampleResponse.script: Starting point: the US retail alcohol market is $70 billion — that's off-premise, the part accessible to delivery. Not all of that is legally addressable. Thirty-eight states permit alcohol delivery, and those states contain approximately 82% of the US adult population. So my legal TAM is $70 billion times 82%, which is $57.4 billion. That's the outer bound of what any US alcohol delivery platform could address today.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Current Market Size
sampleResponse.script: Current alcohol delivery penetration is 4%, which is given. So: $57.4 billion times 4% equals approximately $2.3 billion in current gross alcohol delivery GMV. Quick sanity check — Drizly was the market leader before being shut down in 2024 and peaked at around $750 million in GMV. Add Gopuff, Minibar, and grocery apps already doing alcohol, and $2 to $2.5 billion feels about right. My number holds.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Mature Market Projection
sampleResponse.script: The key strategic question is: what does penetration look like at maturity? Grocery delivery reached 12 to 15 percent penetration after normalizing from COVID. Alcohol has additional friction — regulatory compliance, age verification, liability — so I'll be conservative and model a mature penetration of 10 to 12 percent. At 12%, the mature alcohol delivery market is $57.4 billion times 12%, which equals $6.9 billion in GMV.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Instacart Revenue
sampleResponse.script: Instacart's take rate on grocery is roughly 10 to 15 percent including service fees and delivery margin. Alcohol may carry a slight premium because of liability and compliance costs — I'll use 13%. At $6.9 billion GMV times 13%, Instacart's annual revenue opportunity at full market maturity is approximately $897 million — call it roughly $900 million.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: the current US alcohol delivery market is approximately $2.3 billion in GMV, and the mature opportunity — which is what Instacart would be building toward — is approximately $6.9 billion in GMV, translating to roughly $900 million in net Instacart revenue. Three risks I'd want leadership to weigh before approving the investment: state-by-state licensing is expensive and slow, age verification creates both liability and consumer friction, and with Drizly now shut down, Instacart will need to re-educate the market on alcohol delivery as a habit, which extends the adoption curve by at least 12 to 18 months.

title: Sizing the US Boutique Fitness Studio Market
prompt: ClassPass is pitching to a strategic investor and needs to quantify the total US boutique fitness market (yoga studios, cycling studios, barre, boxing, HIIT) and the addressable share that passes through ClassPass's aggregator model. How would you size this?
context: US gym and fitness studio industry total revenue is approximately $32B. The boutique fitness segment (non-traditional, class-based studios) represents approximately 37% of studio locations but only 28% of total industry revenue due to smaller unit economics. Average boutique class price: $22-35. Active boutique fitness participants nationally: approximately 12M. ClassPass takes 25-30% of each class booking as its fee.
whyThisFramework: SPEAK: State that 12M active boutique participants × average 3-4 classes/month × $28 average price × 12 months = ~$14B gross class spend bottom-up (cross-check against $32B total fitness market × 28% boutique share = $9B top-down). The ~1.5x gap is expected given the 'active participant' sample skews toward enthusiasts; use $9-10B as the reconciled boutique TAM. ClassPass's monetizable TAM = share of participants open to aggregator vs. studio-direct booking × 25-30% take rate.
keyConsiderations: 12M participants × 3.5 classes/month × $28/class × 12 months = ~$14.1B boutique fitness gross spend (bottom-up)
Top-down cross-check: boutique is 28% of $32B total fitness industry = $9B — use this as the more conservative and defensible TAM
ClassPass penetration of boutique spend: assume 18-22% of classes booked through aggregators
Revenue to ClassPass: $9B × 20% aggregator share × 27% take rate = ~$486M revenue TAM; ClassPass at 60-70% of aggregator market = ~$290-340M
clarifyingQuestions: Are we sizing gross consumer spend on boutique fitness classes, or ClassPass's specific revenue opportunity (which is a small fraction of that)?
Should 'boutique fitness' include only studio classes, or also app-based fitness subscriptions like Peloton that compete for the same consumer wallet?
Is the 12M active participant figure current, or pre-COVID — and how much has the market recovered post-2020?
Are we modeling ClassPass's current market share of boutique bookings, or the theoretical TAM if ClassPass expanded its studio network and consumer penetration?

subIssues: How many active boutique fitness participants are there nationally?
What is the average class frequency and price per class?
Does the consumer spend estimate reconcile with the top-down industry revenue figure?
What share of boutique class bookings happen through aggregator platforms vs. direct studio booking?
Which consumer segments are most likely to use ClassPass vs. studio memberships?
Which studio types accept aggregator bookings vs. protect their direct relationships?
What is ClassPass's take rate on each class booking?
How does ClassPass's pricing model (monthly credits) affect realized take rate vs. nominal?
What is ClassPass's current penetration of the aggregator-addressable market?

stepByStep.title: Build bottom-up consumer spend estimate
stepByStep.detail: 12M active boutique participants × 3.5 classes/month (midpoint of a realistic 3-4 class/month range for active studio-goers) × $28 average class price × 12 months = approximately $14.1B in gross boutique class spend. State this clearly, then immediately flag you'll cross-check it against the industry top-down figure.

stepByStep.title: Cross-check with top-down industry data
stepByStep.detail: The $32B total fitness industry × 28% boutique share = $9B. The $14.1B bottom-up estimate is roughly 1.5x the top-down — a modest gap that is expected and easily explained: the 12M 'active participants' skews toward committed studio-goers, pulling the average class frequency above what the typical occasional participant takes. The top-down figure is more defensible for investor purposes.

stepByStep.title: Reconcile and choose best estimate
stepByStep.detail: A reconciled estimate of $9-10B is most defensible — use the top-down $9B as the base and the bottom-up $14B as the ceiling. State you'll proceed with ~$9B as the boutique fitness TAM and briefly explain why the top-down is preferred in ambiguous sizing situations.

stepByStep.title: Identify ClassPass's addressable portion
stepByStep.detail: Not all boutique classes are bookable through aggregators. Estimate 20% of boutique spend passes through aggregator platforms (ClassPass, Mindbody-adjacent apps) vs. direct studio booking or memberships. 20% of $9B = $1.8B in aggregator-mediated spend.

stepByStep.title: Calculate ClassPass revenue
stepByStep.detail: ClassPass takes 25-30% of each booking — use 27% midpoint. $1.8B × 27% = ~$486M as ClassPass's total revenue TAM if it captured all aggregator-mediated boutique spend. ClassPass's actual share of that aggregator market is approximately 60-70%, implying ~$290-340M in current revenue. This is what to present to the strategic investor.

keyTerms.definition: Class-based fitness studios offering specialized formats (yoga, cycling, barre, boxing, HIIT) typically priced at $22-35 per class; distinct from traditional gyms in their class structure and premium positioning
A platform that bundles access to multiple studios under a single subscription, like ClassPass; the studio gives up direct pricing control in exchange for ClassPass-sourced members
ClassPass's fee as a percentage of each class booking value; at 25-30%, ClassPass retains roughly $6-8 of a $28 class booking
A consumer booking directly with the studio — either as a single purchase or membership — bypassing aggregators; the majority of boutique fitness spend still flows this way

A critical market sizing discipline — building the estimate two ways (population × behavior × price vs. total industry revenue × segment share) to test internal consistency and identify overcount; a gap of 1.5x is reconcilable, while a gap of 4x or more signals a flawed assumption that must be corrected

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer explicitly reconciles the bottom-up and top-down estimates, explains the modest gap (enthusiast skew in the active participant count), and builds to ClassPass's specific revenue TAM — not just the gross consumer spend figure. A weak answer uses an implausibly high class frequency (e.g., multiple classes per week) that produces a bottom-up estimate many times larger than the top-down figure, then fails to flag the discrepancy or correct the assumption.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: I'm going to size this in three layers: first, total boutique fitness consumer spend using both a bottom-up and top-down approach; second, the portion of that spend that flows through aggregator platforms like ClassPass; and third, ClassPass's specific revenue opportunity at its take rate. The interesting tension here is reconciling the two approaches, so I'll walk through that explicitly. Ready to proceed?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Quick clarifications: First, are we sizing ClassPass's revenue opportunity, or the total boutique fitness consumer spend? The investor will probably want to see both — the market context and the monetizable slice. Second, should I include app-based competitors like Peloton in my boutique fitness TAM, or just physical studios? I'll stick to physical studios since that's ClassPass's core network.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Bottom-Up Consumer Spend
sampleResponse.script: Starting from the demand side. We're told there are 12 million active boutique fitness participants nationally. A typical active studio-goer takes roughly 3 to 4 classes per month — I'll use 3.5 as my midpoint. At an average class price of $28, which is the midpoint of the $22 to $35 range given, times 12 months, I get: 12 million times 3.5 times $28 times 12, which equals approximately $14 billion in gross boutique fitness consumer spend.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Top-Down Cross-Check
sampleResponse.script: Let me check that against the top-down data. The US fitness industry is $32 billion total, and boutique fitness is 28% of total industry revenue — so that's $32 billion times 28%, which equals approximately $9 billion. My bottom-up says $14 billion, my top-down says $9 billion — a gap of about 1.5 times. That's a reconcilable difference. The most likely explanation is that the 12 million 'active participants' skews toward committed enthusiasts who take classes more frequently than the average person captured in the top-down revenue figure, which includes more casual participants. I'm going to use the $9 billion top-down figure as the more conservative and defensible boutique fitness TAM.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Aggregator-Addressable Portion
sampleResponse.script: Of that $9 billion in boutique class spend, not all of it flows through aggregators. Most participants either buy studio memberships directly or drop in at the door. I'd estimate roughly 20% of boutique spend is aggregator-mediated — that's classes booked through ClassPass, Mindbody partner apps, and similar platforms. Twenty percent of $9 billion equals $1.8 billion in aggregator-addressable spend.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — ClassPass Revenue TAM
sampleResponse.script: ClassPass takes 25 to 30 percent of each booking — I'll use 27% as the midpoint. Applying that to $1.8 billion gives approximately $486 million as ClassPass's revenue TAM if it captured all aggregator-mediated boutique spend. ClassPass is the dominant aggregator and probably holds 60 to 70 percent of that market, which implies current revenue of roughly $290 to $340 million.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize for the strategic investor: the US boutique fitness market is approximately $9 billion in consumer spend, with a bottom-up check around $14 billion that I've discounted for enthusiast overcounting — a normal and expected reconciliation gap. The aggregator-addressable portion — classes where consumers are willing to book through a platform rather than direct — is roughly $1.8 billion. ClassPass's revenue TAM within that is approximately $486 million at 27% take rate, and ClassPass likely captures $290 to $340 million of that today. The growth story is either expanding aggregator penetration from 20% to 30% of boutique spend, or expanding ClassPass's share of aggregator bookings. Both are viable, but expanding aggregator penetration requires changing studio behavior — which is the harder and more defensible lever.

title: Evaluating Old Spice's 'The Man Your Man Could Smell Like' Campaign
prompt: You are a brand consultant reviewing Old Spice's viral 'The Man Your Man Could Smell Like' campaign (Isaiah Mustafa). The campaign launched with a Super Bowl ad and generated 40M YouTube views in the first week. However, Old Spice's sales in the three months following the campaign launch grew only 3% vs. a 9% growth target. How would you evaluate whether this campaign was a success or failure?
context: Old Spice's primary target was women purchasing body wash for male partners — a demographic the brand had previously ignored. Brand awareness increased from 62% to 81% among women 18-34. Trial rate (new purchasers) increased 27% in the campaign window. However, repeat purchase rate among new trial buyers was only 34% at 90 days. The product formulation had not changed; the repositioning was entirely through marketing.
whyThisFramework: Ad Evaluation: reverse-engineer the message ('if he used Old Spice, you'd want him') — was it delivered effectively? Yes, 40M views and awareness lift confirm delivery. But sales underperformance reveals the framework's second question: did the creative work drive purchase behavior or just entertainment? 3C's examines whether the consumer insight (women buy for men) was correct and fully activated.
keyConsiderations: Viral awareness does not equal purchase — the awareness-to-trial gap must be diagnosed
27% trial increase is strong; the 34% repeat rate is the real problem — product must match the promise
The campaign correctly identified women as purchasers — a valid insight well executed creatively
Long-term brand health metrics (awareness, consideration) improved significantly — not just a short-term play
clarifyingQuestions: What was Old Spice's baseline sales growth rate before this campaign — is 3% growth acceleration or deceleration relative to trend?
Is the 9% target a brand-new goal set for this campaign specifically, or a pre-existing business target that would have required strong execution regardless?
Were there any distribution, pricing, or retail execution changes during this period that could explain the sales miss independent of the campaign?
How does the 27% trial rate increase translate to absolute new buyers — what was the base trial rate before the campaign?

subIssues: Was the campaign's core message — 'women should buy Old Spice for their men' — clear and correctly decoded by the target audience?
Did the humor and virality serve the message, or distract from the purchase trigger?
Did the campaign accurately represent what the product delivers?
Did the campaign reach the intended target (women 18-34 purchasing body wash)?
Did the 40M YouTube views translate to awareness lift among the right demographic?
Were retail touchpoints (in-store, online) coordinated with the campaign to capture the awareness spike?
Did the campaign drive trial among new buyers?
Did trial buyers repeat purchase, or was the campaign a one-time curiosity purchase?
Is the 3% sales growth vs. 9% target a campaign failure or a product delivery failure?

stepByStep.title: Assess message clarity and targeting
stepByStep.detail: The campaign targeted women who buy body wash for male partners — a correct and novel insight. The message 'look at your man, now at me' directly called out this buyer and made the benefit explicit. Awareness jump from 62% to 81% among women 18-34 confirms the message reached and registered with the right audience.

stepByStep.title: Evaluate delivery effectiveness
stepByStep.detail: 40M YouTube views in week one and an estimated $163M in earned media value (a widely cited figure from marketing industry analyses of this campaign) are exceptional delivery metrics. The Super Bowl placement amplified reach. Creative delivery worked — this was not a case of a good ad running in the wrong place.

stepByStep.title: Diagnose the trial-to-repeat gap
stepByStep.detail: 27% trial rate increase is strong — the campaign moved people to the shelf. But only 34% of new trial buyers repeated within 90 days. This is the key diagnostic: trial was driven by the campaign, but the product experience failed to retain buyers. The marketing overpromised what the product delivered.

stepByStep.title: Assess long-term brand equity gains
stepByStep.detail: Even with the 90-day sales miss, awareness among women 18-34 increased 19 points — this is a durable asset. Old Spice's brand health improved structurally, setting up long-term category leadership. The campaign should be evaluated on a 12-month sales window, not just the initial 90 days.

stepByStep.title: Deliver a verdict with nuance
stepByStep.detail: The campaign was a brand success and a partial sales failure. The marketing execution was excellent; the product-market fit problem (low repeat rate) is a product and product-experience issue, not an advertising issue. Recommendation: fix product formulation or scent set, not the advertising.

keyTerms.definition: The percentage of new buyers who purchase a product for the first time during a campaign window; Old Spice's trial rate increased 27% — a strong indicator of campaign-driven purchase intent
The share of trial buyers who repurchase within a defined window (here, 90 days); Old Spice's 34% repeat rate is the key warning sign — a healthy CPG repeat rate is typically 40-60%
The percentage of a target audience that recognizes a brand; Old Spice's jump from 62% to 81% among women 18-34 is a 19-point lift — a significant and durable equity gain

Media coverage and social amplification generated organically by a campaign without additional paid placement; Old Spice's viral moment produced an estimated $163M in earned media value according to marketing industry analyses

Whether the physical product experience lives up to the brand promise created by advertising; Old Spice's low repeat rate suggests the product experience did not match the aspirational campaign message

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer separates the campaign's marketing performance (excellent) from the business outcome failure (product repeat issue) and correctly identifies that a 34% repeat rate is the root cause of the sales miss, not the advertising itself. A weak answer simply declares the campaign a failure because sales missed 9%, without diagnosing why.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I declare this a success or failure, I want to frame the evaluation properly. I'm going to look at three dimensions: was the message right, was it delivered effectively, and did it drive the right business outcome? I'll use the ad evaluation framework — which reverse-engineers whether the creative idea matched what the target audience needed to hear — and I'll layer in 3C's to stress-test whether the consumer insight was correct. My hunch is that this campaign succeeded on marketing effectiveness but exposed a product problem. Let me walk through the evidence.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Before I finalize my view, two questions: First, what was Old Spice's sales growth trend before the campaign — was 3% an acceleration or deceleration? If Old Spice was declining before, 3% growth is actually a turnaround. Second, were there any in-store execution changes — shelf placement, promotional pricing, end-cap support — that might have dragged against the campaign? I want to isolate the advertising effect.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Message Evaluation
sampleResponse.script: Let's start with the message. Old Spice correctly identified that women, not men, make the majority of body wash purchase decisions for households. The campaign spoke directly to that buyer with the line 'look at your man, now back to me.' That's a sharp consumer insight, and it was clearly decoded — brand awareness among women 18-34 jumped 19 points, from 62% to 81%. The right audience heard the right message. That part worked.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Delivery Evaluation
sampleResponse.script: On delivery: 40 million YouTube views in the first week plus a Super Bowl launch is exceptional. Industry analyses have estimated the campaign's earned media value at approximately $163 million in week one alone. The campaign was seen and talked about. This is not a case of a good ad running in the wrong place or to the wrong audience. Delivery was executed nearly perfectly.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Business Outcome Diagnosis
sampleResponse.script: Now here's where it gets complicated. Trial increased 27% — so the campaign successfully moved women to the shelf and into the purchase. That's the marketing job, and it was done. But only 34% of those new buyers repurchased within 90 days. A healthy repeat rate for a CPG body wash is typically 45 to 60%. Old Spice's 34% means two-thirds of the new buyers they worked so hard to acquire tried the product once and didn't come back. That is not a marketing problem. That is a product experience problem. The ad promised an aspirational, premium experience. The scent formulation and product experience apparently didn't deliver on that promise for enough buyers.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My verdict: this campaign was a marketing success and a partial business failure — but the failure is not the campaign's fault. Old Spice should not change the advertising. They should change the product. The awareness gain is durable — 19 points among the target demographic doesn't disappear in 90 days. The long-term equity foundation is strong. The action items are: first, conduct product experience research to identify why repeat rates are below benchmark; second, evaluate whether a premium product reformulation or premium SKU extension can retain the new buyers the campaign acquired; third, measure total 12-month sales impact rather than just the 90-day window, because the awareness and trial gains compound over time. Verdict: don't pull the campaign — invest in the product.

title: Post-Crisis Bud Light Advertising Evaluation
prompt: Following the Dylan Mulvaney partnership controversy in April 2023, Bud Light's sales volume declined 26% within six weeks. AB InBev's marketing team has proposed a new campaign called 'Easy to Drink, Easy to Enjoy' featuring traditional outdoor activities and sports. The campaign tests well in focus groups with core Bud Light drinkers (males 25-45, rural/suburban). Should AB InBev approve this campaign, and what would your evaluation criteria be?
context: Bud Light's share of the light beer category fell from 17.1% to 10.3% within four months. Coors Light and Miller Lite captured most of the defectors. The 'Easy to Drink, Easy to Enjoy' campaign is deliberately low-risk and nostalgic, avoiding any cultural commentary. The estimated campaign budget is $400M. Bud Light's research shows 61% of lapsed buyers say they are 'unlikely to return in the next 12 months.'
whyThisFramework: Ad Evaluation reverses engineers the message: 'Bud Light is for regular guys having fun outdoors' — is this the right message for a brand in crisis? 3C's examines whether the lapsed buyer can be won back with nostalgia or has permanently defected. 4P's asks whether advertising alone can solve what is also a distribution (some retailers delisted) and pricing (promotional discounting damaged brand) problem.
keyConsiderations: The message is safe but may be insufficient — lapsed buyers need a reason to forgive and return
61% saying 'unlikely to return' suggests advertising alone won't solve the problem

$400M campaign budget must be evaluated against the revenue impact of the share loss — the US light beer category is approximately $15-20B, making each share point worth roughly $150-200M in annual revenue; Bud Light's 6.8-point loss therefore represents roughly $1.0-1.4B in annual revenue impact
Risk of the low-risk campaign: no emotional hook means no incremental consideration lift
clarifyingQuestions: What does AB InBev define as success for this campaign — share recovery to 17.1%, stopping the decline at 10.3%, or something in between?
Has Bud Light addressed the distribution gaps — some retailers that delisted Bud Light — before spending $400M on advertising to consumers who can't find it on shelf?
Is this campaign intended to win back lapsed buyers (a retention/re-engagement problem) or to hold the remaining 10.3% share and prevent further defection?
What is Coors Light and Miller Lite doing competitively — are they investing to lock in the defectors, or have they gone quiet?

subIssues: Does 'Easy to Drink, Easy to Enjoy' give lapsed buyers a reason to return, or just remind loyalists why they used to drink Bud Light?
Is nostalgia sufficient to overcome an active brand rejection, or does recovery require an explicit acknowledgment of the controversy?
Is the campaign's message differentiated from what Coors Light and Miller Lite are already saying?
Are there distribution gaps (retailer delistings) that advertising cannot solve?
Has promotional discounting damaged Bud Light's price positioning in a way that signals brand distress?
Has the product itself or its retail presentation changed in a way that is relevant to lapsed buyers?
Is $400M a sufficient spend to move the needle on 61% of lapsed buyers who say they won't return?
What share recovery is required to break even on the $400M investment?
Is there a better allocation of $400M — promotions, distribution incentives, events — that would drive faster share recovery?

stepByStep.title: Define success criteria before evaluating the campaign
stepByStep.detail: Before approving or rejecting, establish what 'success' looks like. Is the goal full recovery to 17.1% share? Stopping decline? Winning back a defined subset of lapsed buyers? The evaluation criteria must be anchored to the objective. If the goal is full recovery, this campaign is likely insufficient. If it's to stabilize and hold remaining share, it may be adequate.

stepByStep.title: Evaluate the message for the recovery objective
stepByStep.detail: 'Easy to Drink, Easy to Enjoy' is brand maintenance messaging — it tells existing drinkers they were right to drink Bud Light. It gives lapsed buyers no specific reason to return. For a brand in crisis, recovery advertising typically requires either acknowledging the controversy (the brave path) or making a compelling new reason-to-believe offer. This campaign does neither.

stepByStep.title: Apply 4P's to identify non-advertising problems
stepByStep.detail: Request data on: (a) distribution recovery — how many retailers that delisted Bud Light have restored it? Advertising to consumers who can't find the product is wasteful. (b) Price — has promotional discounting damaged brand perception? Advertising reinforcing quality while the product is on promotion is contradictory. (c) Product/format — any SKU changes that could signal brand renewal?

stepByStep.title: Stress-test the budget ROI
stepByStep.detail: Bud Light lost 6.8 share points (from 17.1% to 10.3%). The US light beer category is approximately $15-20B, making each share point worth roughly $150-200M in annual revenue — so the 6.8-point loss represents approximately $1.0-1.4B in annual revenue impact. Note: this estimate uses external category size data, not information provided in the case. A $400M campaign budget must recover enough share to justify the spend. At roughly $150-200M per share point and a gross margin of approximately 50%, breaking even on the $400M campaign spend requires recovering 4-5 share points in incremental gross profit — a very high bar given that 61% of lapsed buyers say they are unlikely to return.

stepByStep.title: Deliver a conditional recommendation
stepByStep.detail: Conditionally approve the campaign with three modifications: (1) Confirm full distribution restoration before media flights launch; (2) Add a grassroots/experiential element — brand ambassadors at events — that creates personal re-engagement moments rather than passive TV impressions; (3) Set a 6-month share recovery milestone (e.g., 12% share) as a campaign checkpoint to evaluate spend continuation.

keyTerms.definition: A brand's percentage of total category volume or revenue; Bud Light's share fell from 17.1% to 10.3% — a 6.8-point loss. Using external estimates of the US light beer category at approximately $15-20B, each share point is worth roughly $150-200M in annual revenue, implying a revenue impact of approximately $1.0-1.4B from this share loss
A former purchaser who has stopped buying a brand; Bud Light's 61% of lapsed buyers unlikely to return in 12 months means the defection is behavioral, not just attitudinal
The marketing playbook for restoring brand health after a reputational incident; typically requires both message and product action, not advertising alone

Getting a product relisted in retail channels that removed it during a brand crisis; a prerequisite for advertising ROI — ads that drive store visits where the product isn't stocked destroy consumer goodwill

When promotional discounting during a crisis trains consumers to expect a lower price, signaling the brand has been permanently devalued; a risk for Bud Light if deep discounts were used to defend volume

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer does not simply approve or reject the campaign — it reframes the question by identifying what advertising can and cannot solve (distribution gaps, price damage) and proposes specific success criteria and milestones. A weak answer evaluates only the creative concept without raising the 4P's issues or stress-testing whether $400M spend has a positive ROI given 61% lapsed buyer resistance.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I say yes or no to this campaign, I want to reframe the question. The real issue isn't whether this is a good ad — it may be. The issue is whether advertising is the right tool for this stage of brand recovery, and whether this particular message is sufficient. I'm going to evaluate this using ad evaluation to assess the message, 3C's to understand the buyer dynamics, and 4P's to flag whether there are non-advertising problems that $400M in media won't fix. Ready?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Three questions before I go further. One: has distribution been fully restored — are all retailers that delisted Bud Light carrying it again? Because advertising to consumers who can't find the product is worse than not advertising at all. Two: what is the share target for this campaign — stabilize at 10.3%, or begin recovering toward 17.1%? Those require very different campaign strategies. Three: what are Coors Light and Miller Lite spending right now? If they're actively running conquest campaigns targeting Bud Light lapsed buyers, a passive 'Easy to Drink' message won't compete.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Message Evaluation
sampleResponse.script: Let me evaluate the message. 'Easy to Drink, Easy to Enjoy' is a brand maintenance message. It tells existing Bud Light drinkers they made the right choice. It does not give lapsed buyers a reason to return. When 61% of your lapsed buyers say they are unlikely to return in 12 months, you have an active brand rejection on your hands — not passive forgetting. Maintenance messaging doesn't break through active rejection. The campaign is not wrong, it's just insufficient as a recovery play.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — 3C's: Consumer Analysis
sampleResponse.script: From the consumer side: lapsed buyers defected to Coors Light and Miller Lite. Those brands are functionally identical to Bud Light in terms of product. This means the defection is not about product — it's about social signaling. Drinking Bud Light meant something to the core consumer that it no longer means in the same way. A nostalgic outdoor-activities campaign is trying to reclaim that signal, which is the right strategic instinct. The question is whether it's executed with enough emotional intensity to actually move behavior — and a deliberately low-risk, focus-group-safe campaign is unlikely to move people who made an emotionally charged decision to leave.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — 4P's: Non-Advertising Problems
sampleResponse.script: This is the part that concerns me most. Let me run through the four P's quickly. Product — unchanged, so no signal of renewal. Price — if Bud Light has been running deep promotional discounts to protect volume, that's trained consumers to expect a lower price, which signals brand distress and undermines any premium-adjacency in the advertising. Place — if any retailers still haven't relisted Bud Light, launching $400M of advertising creates consumer demand that cannot be fulfilled at retail, which damages trust further. Promotion — the campaign itself. We're spending $400M on promotion while potentially having unresolved product, price, and place issues. That's a misallocated recovery playbook.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: Here's my recommendation: conditionally approve, with three mandatory gates before launch. First, confirm full distribution restoration in all major retail chains — no flight should launch until Bud Light is back on every shelf. Second, do not run this alongside promotional pricing — pick one signal and commit to it; advertising quality while discounting price is contradictory. Third, add a grassroots experiential layer — sponsorships at regional sports events, tailgate activations, local community tie-ins — because passive TV impressions won't move a buyer who made an emotionally active decision to leave. You need personal re-engagement moments. Finally, set a hard 6-month checkpoint: if share has not recovered to 12% by month six, reassess the budget allocation — shift dollars from media to retail incentives and on-premise activation, where conversion rates are faster. The $400M budget is appropriate in scale, but only if the non-advertising fundamentals are fixed first.

title: Evaluating Always' 'Like a Girl' Campaign
prompt: Always launched the 'Like a Girl' campaign in 2014, which redefined the phrase 'run like a girl' as empowering rather than insulting. The campaign won a Cannes Grand Prix and generated 90M YouTube views. P&G's brand manager now needs to present a full campaign evaluation to leadership. How would you evaluate its business effectiveness, and what metrics would you look at?
context: Always holds 26% of the US feminine care market (vs. competitor Kotex at 19%). Among girls under 18, Always' brand consideration increased from 44% to 71% following the campaign. Purchase intent among mothers buying for daughters increased 19%. The campaign launched alongside product unchanged. Always' market share grew from 26% to 28% in the 18 months following the campaign. The feminine care category grew 4% in the period. Note: the US feminine care category is estimated at approximately $8B based on publicly available industry data; this figure is not provided in the case briefing and should be flagged as an external estimate when deriving revenue impact.
whyThisFramework: Ad Evaluation framework: (1) reverse-engineer the message — 'Always aligns with female empowerment' — was it credibly delivered? The Cannes win and cultural impact confirm yes. (2) Evaluate delivery effectiveness: 90M views, brand metrics lift. (3) Assess business outcomes: share grew 2 points. Using the externally estimated ~$8B US feminine care category, 2 share points equals approximately $160M in incremental annual revenue — flag this as an estimated calculation. 3C's validates that the competitive framing (vs. Kotex) was sound.
keyConsiderations: The message was purpose-driven but earned a business result — 2-share-point gain is significant
Brand consideration among teens (future lifetime buyers) is a long-term strategic win
Creative bravery was rewarded — the campaign reframed cultural language, not just product benefits
Success metrics beyond sales: cultural impact, PR value, award credibility all build equity
clarifyingQuestions: How should we attribute the 2-share-point gain — entirely to the campaign, or also to distribution changes or trade promotions during the period?
Is the primary success metric brand equity (awareness, consideration) or pure sales/share growth — since the campaign seems to have achieved both?
