Should we evaluate this campaign on its 18-month results, or does the long-term investment in teen consideration (ages under 18) require a longer measurement window?
How does Always define 'category growth' — unit volume or dollar value — given the 4% category growth in the period?

subIssues: Was 'Like a Girl' as empowerment the right message for Always to own credibly?
Did the campaign's purpose-driven message align with Always' product category and brand position?
Was the emotional insight — that puberty damages girls' confidence — relevant to Always' core buyer?
Did the 90M YouTube views reach the intended audience — teen girls and mothers?
Did the Cannes Grand Prix amplify credibility with media buyers and retail partners?
Was the earned media coverage proportionate to the paid media investment?
Did share grow, and by how much in absolute dollar terms?
Was the share gain organic (incremental buyers) or defensive (retained at-risk buyers)?
Did the campaign drive disproportionate gains among teens — the future lifetime value cohort?

stepByStep.title: Evaluate the strategic message choice
stepByStep.detail: Always chose to connect to female empowerment at a category-defining moment — puberty. This is a genuine brand truth: Always is there for girls at their most vulnerable moment. The message 'Like a Girl = strength' is credible from Always in a way it wouldn't be from a snack brand. Message-category fit is excellent.

stepByStep.title: Assess delivery effectiveness
stepByStep.detail: 90M YouTube views plus Cannes Grand Prix equals exceptional delivery. Cultural penetration — the phrase 'like a girl' was permanently reclaimed — is a rare campaign outcome. The earned media value of this cultural moment far exceeds any paid media investment. Delivery grade: exceptional.

stepByStep.title: Quantify the business outcome
stepByStep.detail: Market share moved from 26% to 28% in 18 months — a 2-point gain. To translate this to dollars, you need the category size, which is not provided in the case. Using publicly available industry estimates, the US feminine care category is approximately $8B; on that basis, 2 points of share equals approximately $160M in incremental annual revenue. Flag this as an external estimate and ask if the client can confirm the category size. The feminine care category grew 4% overall, so Always' gain exceeds category growth, meaning it took share from competitors (primarily Kotex, at 19%).

stepByStep.title: Evaluate the long-term equity investment
stepByStep.detail: Brand consideration among girls under 18 jumped from 44% to 71% — a 27-point gain in the future lifetime buyer cohort. This cohort represents 30+ years of potential purchase loyalty. Even without a precise LTV calculation — which would require knowing average annual category spend and retention rates — the directional insight is clear: acquiring loyal teen buyers at the beginning of their category journey is the highest-value outcome of this campaign and worth multiples of the campaign's media spend.

stepByStep.title: Deliver a clear verdict
stepByStep.detail: The campaign is a clear success on all dimensions: message credibility, delivery reach, short-term share gain, and long-term equity investment in the next generation of buyers. The only risk to flag: maintaining campaign relevance over time — purpose-driven campaigns can become formulaic if not evolved. Recommend extending the platform with fresh executions rather than repeating the original.

keyTerms.definition: The percentage of target buyers who would consider purchasing a brand — a leading indicator of future sales; Always' teen consideration jumped 27 points to 71%

A brand's percentage of total category sales; Always grew from 26% to 28% share — a 2-point gain. Using an external industry estimate of approximately $8B for the US feminine care category, this 2-point gain is worth approximately $160M in annual incremental revenue (note: category size is not given in the case and should be flagged as an estimate)

Advertising that connects a brand to a social value or cause beyond product benefits; 'Like a Girl' was purpose-driven because it addressed female confidence at puberty — a real tension the brand's category is adjacent to

The long-term revenue from a group of buyers acquired at the same time; teen girls recruited as Always buyers at age 13-17 represent 30+ years of potential purchase loyalty — making the consideration gain among under-18s the highest-value metric in this campaign

Organic press coverage, social sharing, and cultural conversation generated by a campaign without additional paid distribution; the 'Like a Girl' cultural moment generated earned media far exceeding the paid campaign investment

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer correctly identifies the teen consideration metric as the most strategically important result — not just the share gain — and frames the campaign as a long-term equity investment in addition to a short-term sales win. A strong answer also flags that translating the 2-point share gain into dollars requires an external category size estimate not given in the case. A weak answer focuses only on the Cannes award and YouTube views without attempting to quantify the revenue impact of the share gain or identifying the LTV implications of the teen consideration lift.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: I'm going to evaluate this campaign on three dimensions: was the message right for Always to own, was it delivered effectively, and what were the business outcomes — both short-term share and long-term equity? I'll use the ad evaluation framework to structure this, and I'll bring in 3C's to check the competitive framing. My thesis going in: this campaign succeeded on all three dimensions, which is rare — and I want to be precise about what 'success' means in dollar terms.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Two questions before I begin. First — should I attribute the full 2-share-point gain to the campaign, or are there confounding factors like distribution gains or promotions during the same period? For now, I'll assume it was primarily campaign-driven and flag if I need to hedge. Second — do we have the total US feminine care category size? It's not in the briefing, and I'll need to use an external estimate to put the share gain in dollar terms. I'll use approximately $8 billion, which is consistent with publicly available industry data, and flag that figure as an estimate.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Message Credibility
sampleResponse.script: Start with the message: 'Always aligns with female empowerment by reclaiming the phrase Like a Girl.' The critical question in purpose-driven campaigns is whether the brand has the right to own the message. Always absolutely does. This is a brand that is with girls at their most vulnerable moment — the beginning of puberty. The insight that confidence drops dramatically in girls at that age is both true and directly adjacent to the category occasion Always serves. The message is credible in a way that it wouldn't be from, say, a snack brand running a female empowerment campaign. Message-category fit: excellent.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Delivery Effectiveness
sampleResponse.script: On delivery: 90 million YouTube views plus a Cannes Grand Prix. But more importantly, the campaign achieved something very rare — it permanently changed cultural language. The phrase 'like a girl' has been reclaimed in everyday conversation, in media, in sports commentary. That level of cultural penetration is nearly impossible to buy with paid media. The earned media value — press coverage, social sharing, cultural commentary — likely dwarfs the paid campaign investment. Delivery: exceptional.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Business Outcomes
sampleResponse.script: Now the financial results. Market share grew from 26% to 28% in 18 months — that's 2 points of share. The case doesn't give us the absolute category size, so I'll use an external industry estimate of approximately $8 billion for US feminine care. Two points of share at that scale equals approximately $160 million in incremental annual revenue — and I'd flag that number as an estimate that leadership should verify with the actual category data. The category also grew 4% in this period, meaning Always' gain exceeds category growth — it took share from competitors, not just rode the category tide. The most likely donor: Kotex, which sits at 19% share.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Long-Term Equity: The Teen Cohort
sampleResponse.script: But here's the metric I'd actually lead with in the leadership presentation: brand consideration among girls under 18 jumped from 44% to 71% — a 27-point gain. Why is this the most important number? Because a girl who forms brand loyalty to Always at age 14 is likely to remain a customer for 30-plus years. The case doesn't give us average annual category spend, so I won't invent a precise LTV figure — but the directional point is clear: acquiring loyal teen buyers at the start of their category journey is worth multiples of the campaign's media spend, and the 27-point consideration gain among millions of teenage girls is the campaign's most durable asset.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: Verdict: this campaign is an unqualified success across all dimensions. Message credibility is high, delivery is exceptional, short-term share grew approximately $160 million in estimated incremental annual revenue, and the long-term teen equity investment may be the most valuable outcome of all. The one risk I'd flag for leadership: purpose-driven platforms can become formulaic if recycled without evolution. 'Like a Girl' worked because it felt surprising and genuine in 2014. Future executions must evolve the platform rather than repeat it, or it will feel performative. My recommendation is to extend the platform with fresh creative executions — new settings, new athletes, new moments — rather than retreating to product-benefit advertising that would abandon the equity built.

title: Evaluating Dove's 'Real Beauty' Campaign Longevity
prompt: Dove's 'Real Beauty' campaign launched in 2004 and is now 20 years old. Unilever's board is questioning whether the campaign has run its course, noting that Dove's US market share in body wash has declined from 17% to 14% over the past four years despite continued 'Real Beauty' advertising. A challenger brand (Native, at 8% share) is growing 18% annually. Should Unilever refresh, extend, or retire 'Real Beauty'?
context: Dove's brand equity scores on 'stands for something meaningful' remain high (81% agree) but 'brand feels fresh and current' has declined from 74% to 61% in four years. Gen Z (18-26) shows 38% lower Dove brand affinity than Millennials. Native's success is driven by ingredient transparency and DTC model. Dove's Real Beauty messaging still resonates strongly with 35-55 year old women. Unilever has invested $60M annually in Real Beauty advertising.
whyThisFramework: Ad Evaluation diagnoses the campaign: message (empowering authentic beauty) is still resonating with core 35-55 consumers, but delivery is failing with Gen Z — they find it dated or preachy. 3C's identifies Native as the competitive threat with a different value proposition (ingredients > body image). STP asks whether Dove needs to segment its messaging by generation rather than a single universal Real Beauty narrative.
keyConsiderations: 20-year campaigns can win loyalty among core users but fail to recruit next-generation buyers
Gen Z cares about ingredients and sustainability more than body image empowerment — different insight needed
The campaign itself is not failing; it is aging out of relevance with new entrants
Strategic options: segment messaging (Real Beauty for 35+, ingredient-led for Gen Z), or refresh platform entirely
clarifyingQuestions: Is Dove's 3-point share loss driven by losing existing buyers to competitors, or by failing to recruit new Gen Z buyers as they enter the category?
Does Unilever's $60M annual Real Beauty budget cover all body wash advertising, or is there additional product/ingredient-focused media running alongside the brand platform?
What is Native's price positioning relative to Dove — is the share loss a value trade-down or a premium trade-up?
Has Dove tested any ingredient-focused or sustainability-led messaging with Gen Z, or has the entire brand voice been 'Real Beauty' for the past 20 years?

subIssues: Is 'Real Beauty' still resonating with Dove's core 35-55 consumer, and driving purchase retention?
Is 'Real Beauty' failing to recruit Gen Z buyers, and why specifically?
Is the campaign's message being delivered in the channels Gen Z actually uses?
Is Native winning on a different value proposition (ingredients, sustainability) rather than competing on body image messaging?
Are Dove's share losses concentrated among younger buyers who skew toward Native?
Can Dove address the Native threat within 'Real Beauty' or does it require a new platform?
Should Dove segment messaging — maintain 'Real Beauty' for 35-55, launch an ingredient-led platform for Gen Z?
Should Dove refresh 'Real Beauty' with Gen Z-relevant executions while keeping the platform?
Should Dove retire 'Real Beauty' and build an entirely new brand platform for the next decade?

stepByStep.title: Diagnose whether the campaign or the strategy is the problem
stepByStep.detail: Separate the question: is 'Real Beauty' failing (wrong message) or aging (right message for the wrong segment)? Data shows it still resonates at 81% on 'stands for something meaningful' — so the message is not wrong. But 'brand feels fresh' dropped 13 points, and Gen Z affinity is 38% lower than Millennials. The platform is correct; the targeting and execution are the problem.

stepByStep.title: Apply STP: is Dove trying to be all things to all women?
stepByStep.detail: A 20-year-old campaign that was built on Millennial and Gen X insights is now running to Gen Z with the same message. STP asks: who is the target, what do they want, and how is Dove positioned against that need? Gen Z wants ingredient transparency and sustainability — not a body image conversation. Real Beauty doesn't speak to that.

stepByStep.title: Analyze the Native threat through 3C's
stepByStep.detail: Native (8% share, 18% growth) is winning on a completely different value proposition: ingredient clarity ('no parabens, no sulfates'), DTC transparency, and sustainability ethos. This is not a Real Beauty vs. Real Beauty competition — it's an entirely different conversation. Dove cannot out-Native Native with Real Beauty messaging.

stepByStep.title: Build the strategic options and evaluate
stepByStep.detail: Three options: (1) Retire Real Beauty — highest risk, destroys equity with loyal 35-55 core; (2) Segment messaging — maintain Real Beauty for 35-55, launch an ingredient-transparency platform for Gen Z under the Dove masterbrand; (3) Refresh Real Beauty executions for Gen Z channels (TikTok, Instagram) while keeping the brand theme. Option 2 is most defensible: protect core equity while recruiting the next generation.

stepByStep.title: Recommend with investment allocation
stepByStep.detail: Recommend Option 2 with a 60/40 budget split: $36M maintaining Real Beauty for the core 35-55 segment, $24M launching an ingredient-transparency and sustainability platform for Gen Z. Measure Gen Z trial and consideration at 12 months. Do not retire Real Beauty — the equity score of 81% for 'stands for something meaningful' is too valuable to abandon.

keyTerms.definition: How long a campaign idea remains effective before it becomes either dated or so familiar it loses impact; 'Real Beauty' at 20 years is one of the longest-running brand platforms in CPG history

The difference in brand affinity between younger and older cohorts; Dove's 38% Gen Z affinity gap vs. Millennials signals a recruitment failure that compounds as older loyalists age out of the category
Brand positioning built on product formula transparency ('no parabens, sulfate-free, naturally derived') rather than emotional or social benefit; Native's primary competitive weapon against Dove
Running distinct messages to different consumer segments under the same masterbrand; the recommended strategy for Dove — Real Beauty for 35-55, ingredient-transparency for Gen Z
The overarching parent brand (Dove) under which different product lines or messaging platforms exist; maintaining masterbrand equity while evolving segment messaging is the core strategic challenge

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer clearly distinguishes between the campaign message failing and the campaign aging out of relevance with a new generation, proposes a concrete segmentation strategy, and does not simply say 'retire' or 'keep' without reasoning. A weak answer treats the 3-point share loss as proof the campaign has failed without diagnosing whether Gen Z recruitment or existing buyer defection is the driver.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I recommend retiring, refreshing, or extending, I want to separate two questions: is Real Beauty the wrong message, or is it the right message aging out of relevance with a new generation of buyers? Those have very different strategic responses. I'll use ad evaluation to diagnose the campaign's performance by segment, STP to understand whether Dove is trying to be all things to all women, and 3C's to understand the Native competitive threat specifically. Ready?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Three questions. First: is Dove's 3-point share loss driven by existing buyers leaving, or by failing to recruit Gen Z as they enter the category? The distinction matters — retention and acquisition require different fixes. Second: has Dove tested any ingredient-focused messaging with Gen Z, or has the entire brand been 'Real Beauty' for 20 years? Third: is Native winning on price, ingredients, or both — because if it's a value trade-down, that's a different problem than a premium trade-up.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Is Real Beauty Failing or Aging?
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with the data. Dove's 'stands for something meaningful' score is still 81% — that is an exceptionally strong equity score for any brand after 20 years. The message is not broken. But 'brand feels fresh and current' dropped from 74% to 61% in four years, and Gen Z affinity is 38% below Millennials. That tells me Real Beauty is not a failed campaign — it's an aging campaign. It built profound equity with its original audience and is now failing to recruit the next generation. The campaign itself is correct; the targeting and channel execution are the problem.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — STP: Is Dove Targeting Everyone and Reaching No One?
sampleResponse.script: Here's the STP issue. A campaign built on 2004 insights about Millennial and Gen X women's relationship with beauty standards is now running on TikTok to Gen Z buyers as if nothing has changed. But Gen Z's relationship with beauty is different — they care about ingredient transparency, sustainability, and brand authenticity defined by supply chain ethics, not body image. Real Beauty speaks to 'your body is beautiful as it is.' Gen Z is asking 'what's actually in this bottle?' Those are different conversations, and Dove is answering the wrong question for the younger segment.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — 3C's: The Native Threat
sampleResponse.script: Native is at 8% share and growing 18% annually. That's a brand that will be at approximately 17% share within five years if the trajectory holds — which would match Dove today. But Native is not competing on Real Beauty's terms. Native is winning on ingredient lists: no parabens, no sulfates, naturally derived, cruelty-free. That's a completely different value proposition. Dove cannot defeat Native by running more Real Beauty campaigns. Dove needs an ingredient-transparency response — which means either a product reformulation story or a new communication platform.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Strategic Options
sampleResponse.script: Three options on the table. Option one: retire Real Beauty. High risk — Dove's 81% equity score on 'stands for something meaningful' is a 20-year asset you cannot recreate. Retiring it abandons the 35-55 core who still respond and still buy. I'd rule this out. Option two: refresh Real Beauty for Gen Z channels — run the same brand platform but with new executions, new talent, new formats built for TikTok and Instagram. Moderate effectiveness — Gen Z may still find the body-image conversation dated or performative regardless of format. Option three: segment the messaging. Maintain Real Beauty for the 35-55 core, invest in an ingredient-transparency and sustainability platform for Gen Z under the Dove masterbrand. Different messages, same brand. This is what I recommend.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My recommendation is Option Three: segment the brand messaging. Allocate the $60 million annual budget as 60-40 — $36 million maintaining Real Beauty for the 35-55 core consumer, and $24 million launching a new Gen Z platform built on ingredient transparency, sustainability credentials, and DTC-native formats. The Gen Z platform should explicitly address the Native threat: Dove's ingredients, Dove's supply chain, Dove's commitment to no harmful chemicals. Measure Gen Z trial and brand consideration at 12 months post-launch as the success gate. Do not retire Real Beauty — an 81% equity score for 'stands for something meaningful' is irreplaceable. Evolve the brand, don't abandon it.

title: Evaluating Nike's 'Dream Crazy' Colin Kaepernick Campaign
prompt: Nike launched its 'Dream Crazy' campaign featuring Colin Kaepernick in September 2018. Within 48 hours of launch, #BoycottNike was trending, with videos of consumers burning Nike products. Nike's stock declined 3% on the day of launch. However, Nike's digital sales increased 31% in the week following the launch. You are Nike's CMO presenting a 6-month campaign evaluation. How do you frame this?
context: Nike's sales in the six months following the campaign grew 10% YoY vs. 7% in the prior period. Nike's brand consideration among 15-24 year olds increased 5 points. Among consumers 45+, brand consideration declined 4 points. Nike's research shows the target of 'the next generation of athletes' (15-25 year olds) responded overwhelmingly positively. Earned media from the campaign was valued at approximately $86M in the first week (based on Apex Marketing Group's tracking methodology); estimates vary by source and methodology.
whyThisFramework: Ad Evaluation: reverse-engineer the message — 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything' positions Nike as a brand with conviction, aligning with Gen Z/young Millennial values. Was it delivered effectively? Boycotts from an unintended audience prove it cut through. STP is the second lens: the campaign was precisely targeted at youth athletes, and for that segment, it worked measurably.
keyConsiderations: Short-term stock decline and boycott noise are not campaign failure metrics — they are signal
The campaign succeeded for its intended target (15-25 year olds) and failed with non-targets (45+)
Earned media in week one far exceeds a typical paid media buy of equivalent scale
The willingness to alienate some consumers to deeply connect with the target is the strategic insight
clarifyingQuestions: Is Nike's CMO presenting this as a success or is leadership genuinely uncertain — because the framing of this presentation requires knowing what narrative we're defending?
How much was the paid media investment for this campaign — because the earned media comparison only makes sense with the paid budget as a reference point?
Is the 3% stock decline on launch day an isolated data point, or did Nike's stock recover and exceed pre-campaign levels within the 6-month evaluation window?
Should we isolate the Kaepernick campaign's effect on the 10% revenue growth, or is there product line momentum (Jordan Brand, new shoe launches) that is confounding the attribution?

subIssues: Was 'Believe in something even if it means sacrificing everything' the right message for Nike to own, given its brand heritage?
Did the message resonate with the 15-25 target in measurable ways (consideration, intent, purchase)?
Was Kaepernick the right messenger — credible, admired, and relevant to the target's values?
Was the boycott from 45+ consumers a predictable and acceptable cost of the campaign?
Did the stock decline reflect permanent value destruction or a temporary noise reaction?
Did Nike manage the controversy in a way that deepened target loyalty rather than retreating?
Did 6-month revenue growth of 10% exceed baseline trend, and what portion is attributable to the campaign?
Did the 31% digital sales spike in week one convert to sustained purchase behavior?
Is the consideration gain among 15-24 year olds a durable asset or a short-term spike?

stepByStep.title: Frame the evaluation lens correctly
stepByStep.detail: Open by establishing the right evaluation criteria: a campaign should be judged by its performance with the intended target, not its reception by non-targets. Nike's target was 15-25 year old athletes. All metrics should be anchored to that group. The boycott from 45+ consumers is not a campaign failure — it is evidence the campaign had sharp targeting.

stepByStep.title: Evaluate message credibility
stepByStep.detail: 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything.' This message required Nike to actually take a position — sacrificing short-term sales with some consumers. Nike's willingness to do so made the message credible. A brand that says 'believe' but doesn't show belief is performative. Nike showed belief, which is why the target trusted it.

stepByStep.title: Assess campaign delivery and earned media ROI
stepByStep.detail: Earned media estimates for the campaign's first week vary by source and methodology — Apex Marketing Group estimated approximately $86M. The controversy was the amplification mechanism. Without the boycott noise, the campaign would have received normal media coverage. The controversy — which Nike anticipated — turned a campaign into a cultural moment. This is a sophisticated use of polarization as earned media strategy.

stepByStep.title: Quantify business outcomes by segment
stepByStep.detail: Target segment (15-24): brand consideration up 5 points — a durable asset. Non-target (45+): consideration down 4 points — an acceptable cost, particularly since this segment has lower lifetime value and lower athletic product purchase frequency. 6-month revenue: 10% growth vs. 7% prior period baseline = 3 points of incremental growth. Digital spike of 31% in week one shows purchase intent was immediate.

stepByStep.title: Address the stock decline and close
stepByStep.detail: The 3% stock decline was a one-day market reaction to controversy noise — an efficient market overreacting to social media sentiment. Present the 6-month stock trajectory data (Nike's stock recovered in the months following the campaign, though the specific timeline and extent of recovery should be confirmed with the actual stock data). Revenue and long-term brand equity tell the real story.

keyTerms.definition: The deliberate choice to take a position that will alienate some consumers in order to deeply connect with the intended target; Nike's Kaepernick campaign accepted 45+ consumer backlash to win deep loyalty among 15-25 year old athletes

The ratio of organic media coverage generated by a campaign to its paid media investment; Nike's campaign generated substantial earned media in week one — estimates vary by methodology — vastly multiplying the paid investment through controversy-driven coverage
The share of a target audience that would consider purchasing a brand; Nike's 5-point gain among 15-24 year olds and 4-point loss among 45+ shows precise targeting effectiveness

The tension between immediate sales/stock performance and durable brand equity; the Kaepernick campaign sacrificed short-term stock price for long-term positioning as the brand of the next generation of athletes

Applying Segmentation-Targeting-Positioning to assess whether a campaign served its intended segment; Nike's campaign was precisely targeted at 15-25 year olds and the metrics confirm it succeeded with exactly that group

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer immediately reframes the 'failure' narrative by anchoring evaluation to the intended target, correctly interprets the 45+ backlash as evidence of sharp targeting rather than a problem, and translates the business outcomes into a clear ROI case. A weak answer treats the stock decline and #BoycottNike as evidence of campaign failure without distinguishing target vs. non-target reactions.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I present the 6-month results, I want to establish the right evaluation frame — because some of you will have walked in here thinking the boycott proves this was a mistake. It doesn't. A campaign should be judged by its performance with the intended target, not its reception by people who were never the target. Nike's target was the next generation of athletes: 15 to 25 year olds. Every metric I'm about to present is measured against that audience. The 45-and-over consumer reaction is a separate signal — and I'll address it, but it's not the evaluation criteria. I'll use the ad evaluation framework to assess message, delivery, and business outcomes, and I'll use STP to validate that the targeting was precise. Let's go.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Two questions before I present. First — does leadership have the 6-month stock price data? The 3% launch-day decline is the metric the board fixates on, but I want to show the full recovery curve. Second — do we have the paid media investment figure, so I can calculate the earned media multiplier? The week-one earned media is the headline number, but it's even more powerful when set against the paid spend.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Was the Message Right?
sampleResponse.script: Let's start with the message: 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything.' This is not a product campaign — it's a values campaign. The question is whether Nike had the credibility to own a values statement. I'd argue yes, for two reasons. First, Nike's entire brand DNA is built on the athlete who pushes past limits — Just Do It. Believing in something despite sacrifice is the athletic metaphor extended to civil life. Second — and this is the critical part — Nike actually demonstrated belief by running this campaign despite knowing it would cost them something. A brand that says 'believe' but never believes itself is hollow. Nike believed, and that made the message credible to the target.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Delivery and Earned Media
sampleResponse.script: On delivery: the campaign generated substantial earned media in its first week — estimates from tracking firms vary, but the scale was significant relative to a typical paid buy. The controversy — #BoycottNike, the burning products, the cable news coverage — didn't hurt delivery. It was the delivery. Nike anticipated that a polarizing position would generate polarizing coverage, and polarizing coverage is free media at scale. The burning shoes looked like destruction; to a 17-year-old athlete watching, it looked like adults who don't understand them proving that Nike does.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Target Segment Results
sampleResponse.script: Results for the intended target: 15 to 24 year olds, brand consideration up 5 points. That is a significant, durable brand equity gain in the highest-value future buyer cohort. Digital sales up 31% in the week of launch — purchase intent was immediate and expressed at the register, not just in surveys. Six-month revenue growth of 10% year-over-year versus 7% in the prior period — that's 3 points of incremental growth, and at Nike's revenue scale, 3 points is worth hundreds of millions of dollars in absolute terms.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Non-Target Reaction in Context
sampleResponse.script: Now the 45-plus consumer: brand consideration down 4 points. I want to be direct: this was expected, it was acceptable, and it is not a symmetric loss. The 45-plus consumer purchases athletic apparel at lower frequency and has lower long-term value to Nike's growth equation. The 15-24 year old is the buyer Nike needs to lock in for the next 30 years. Trading 4 points of consideration with the non-target for 5 points of consideration with the target is a net-positive exchange. If we had run a campaign that held flat with 45-plus and moved nothing with 15-24, that would have been the failure. As for the stock: the 3-percent launch-day decline was market noise — an overreaction to social media sentiment. The stock recovered in the months following the campaign — the specific recovery timeline should be confirmed with the actual stock data, but the market's direction of travel agreed with Nike's strategic judgment.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My six-month verdict: this campaign is a clear success by every metric that matters. Message credibility: earned. Delivery: substantial earned media in week one, amplified by the controversy itself. Target performance: 5-point consideration gain, 31% digital sales spike, 10% six-month revenue growth. The controversy was not collateral damage — it was the mechanism. Nike took a position, the market tested that position, and the target responded with their wallets. The recommendation going forward: do not walk this back, do not soften the brand voice, and do not chase the 45-plus consumer with follow-up campaigns that signal retreat. That would signal to the 15-24 year old that Nike's conviction was a marketing stunt. Stay the course, deepen the athlete partnership pipeline, and measure 24-month consideration trends to confirm the equity gain is compounding. This campaign will be in marketing case studies for the next 20 years — and not as a cautionary tale.

title: Nike Women's Running Growth
prompt: You are a senior brand manager at Nike. Women's running apparel is growing 12% annually as a category, but Nike's women's running revenue has grown only 4% over the same period. Nike's overall women's business is $8B globally. What strategic direction would you recommend to close the gap?
context: Lululemon has become the dominant player in women's athletic apparel with 28% of the women's premium segment. Nike's women's running share has dropped from 31% to 24% over 3 years. Consumer research shows Nike is perceived as performance-first but less aligned with the 'wellness lifestyle' trend driving growth. Nike's women's marketing spend is 18% of total marketing budget vs. women representing 40% of athletic apparel buyers.
whyThisFramework: 3C's to diagnose why Nike is undergrowing the category (consumer perception shift, competitive positioning vs. Lululemon, company resource allocation), then STP to redesign targeting and positioning for the underserved wellness-oriented segment.
keyConsiderations: Why are women choosing Lululemon over Nike — performance vs. lifestyle positioning gap
Is this a product problem, a marketing problem, or a distribution problem?
What segments within women's running are growing fastest?
How does Nike leverage its scale without diluting its performance brand?
clarifyingQuestions: When we say women's running revenue grew 4%, is that units, dollars, or both — and how does that break down between footwear, apparel, and accessories?
Is the 31%-to-24% share loss concentrated in a particular price tier or channel, like DTC vs. wholesale?
How does Nike's women's running marketing budget of 18% compare to two or three years ago — has this been declining, flat, or is this a new gap?
Do we have any data on where lost Nike women's running customers are going — is it primarily Lululemon, or are other brands like Athleta or On Running capturing share?

subIssues: Are women's running buyers choosing wellness-lifestyle positioning over performance positioning, and is this a permanent shift?
What specific product attributes or brand signals are driving the perception that Nike is 'not for me'?
Which segments — casual wellness runners, competitive runners, athleisure wearers — are growing fastest and where is Nike weakest?
On what dimensions — price, product, community, retail experience — is Lululemon winning that Nike can realistically compete on?
Is Lululemon's advantage structural (brand identity, community) or tactical (product assortment, marketing)?
Are there segments or use cases Lululemon doesn't serve well where Nike could establish a beachhead?
Is the 18% marketing budget allocation for women the root cause of underperformance, and what would parity (40%) unlock?
Are Nike's design and product development pipelines structured to serve women's wellness needs, or are they adapting men's products?
Which channel — DTC, wholesale, Nike app — has the highest share of women's running and where is the biggest growth leverage?

stepByStep.title: Quantify the growth gap
stepByStep.detail: Establish the dollar gap: if the category grew 12% and Nike grew 4%, on an $8B women's base Nike underperformed by roughly $640M in foregone revenue relative to category growth. Ask for a breakdown by product type (footwear vs. apparel) and channel to find where underperformance is sharpest.

stepByStep.title: Diagnose the consumer perception gap
stepByStep.detail: Request the consumer perception data comparing Nike to Lululemon on key attributes: performance, lifestyle alignment, community, style, and value. The 'performance-first vs. wellness lifestyle' gap should be quantified — what % of women's running buyers rank lifestyle factors above performance when choosing a brand?

stepByStep.title: Segment and target
stepByStep.detail: Apply STP to divide women's running into three segments: competitive performance runners (small, Nike-strong), casual wellness/lifestyle runners (large, growing, Lululemon-dominant), and athleisure crossover buyers (large, fragmented). Recommend targeting the lifestyle/wellness segment as the growth priority with a dedicated sub-brand or repositioning campaign.

stepByStep.title: Develop the positioning and marketing reallocation plan
stepByStep.detail: Recommend repositioning Nike women's running from 'athlete performance' to 'empowered movement' — a bridge between performance and wellness. Recommend increasing women's marketing budget toward 30-35% of total over two years, funding a women-specific brand campaign, community-based marketing (running clubs, studio partnerships), and product storytelling around comfort and style alongside performance.

stepByStep.title: Recommend channel and product actions
stepByStep.detail: Prioritize DTC and Nike app as primary channels for women's running since they allow curated, full-brand storytelling. Propose a 3-to-5 product launches specifically designed for the wellness runner archetype — versatile, stylish, performance-capable — differentiated from Lululemon on Nike's technology heritage (Flyknit, Dri-FIT) while communicating aesthetic and lifestyle appeal.

keyTerms.definition: Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning — a three-step framework to identify which customer group to pursue, then craft a message specifically for them
A brand's percentage of total category sales; Nike's women's running share dropped from 31% to 24%, meaning competitors took those dollars
Marketing a product as part of an aspirational way of living rather than on functional performance specs — Lululemon's core advantage over Nike in women's apparel
Selling directly through Nike's own stores and app rather than through retailers like Foot Locker — higher margins and full brand control
How a company organizes its brands and sub-brands; Nike must decide whether to reposition the core Nike brand for women or create a dedicated sub-brand

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer immediately quantifies the gap in dollar terms, uses 3C's to structure the diagnosis before jumping to solutions, and makes a specific recommendation about which consumer segment to target and how to reallocate marketing spend. A weak answer jumps to tactics (launch a new product line) without diagnosing whether the root cause is product, perception, or marketing investment.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Thank you — this is a classic category-underperformance problem, and I'd like to use a two-part framework. First I'll apply the 3C's — Consumer, Competition, and Company — to diagnose why Nike's women's running revenue is growing at 4% when the category is growing at 12%. Then I'll use STP to design the strategic response. Before I dive in, may I ask a couple of clarifying questions?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: First, when we say Nike's women's running grew 4%, is that concentrated in a specific product category — footwear, apparel — or is it broad-based? And second, do we have any data on where lost customers are going — is it primarily Lululemon, or is this fragmented across multiple competitors? Great, thank you. I'll proceed with those assumptions.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — 3C's Diagnosis
sampleResponse.script: Starting with the Consumer: the consumer research tells us there's a perception gap — Nike reads as 'performance-first' while the growth in women's running is being driven by a 'wellness lifestyle' buyer who wants to feel good, look good, and be part of a community. That buyer is choosing Lululemon. On Competition: Lululemon has 28% of the women's premium segment, and they've built that through community marketing, premium retail experience, and lifestyle positioning that Nike hasn't matched. On the Company side: the most actionable data point is that Nike is directing only 18% of total marketing spend at women, who represent 40% of athletic apparel buyers. That is a structural underinvestment that compounds the perception problem.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — STP Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: Now applying STP: I'd segment women's running into three groups — competitive performance runners, casual wellness runners, and athleisure crossover buyers. The wellness runner segment is where the 12% category growth is concentrated, and it's where Nike is weakest relative to Lululemon. My recommendation is to target that wellness segment with a repositioned sub-line — call it Nike Move or Nike Wellrun — that bridges Nike's performance technology with lifestyle aesthetics. The positioning would be: 'made for every mile of your day,' communicating that Nike products perform when you run and feel great when you don't. Operationally, I'd increase women's marketing budget from 18% to at least 30% over two years, invest in women's running communities and studio partnerships the way Lululemon has, and prioritize DTC channels where Nike controls the full brand story.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: In summary, the root cause of Nike's 4% growth vs. a 12% category is a combination of underinvestment in women's marketing — 18% budget for 40% of buyers — and a performance-only brand perception that doesn't resonate with the wellness runner driving category growth. The strategic recommendation is threefold: reposition Nike women's running around 'empowered movement,' double down on marketing investment toward the 30-35% range, and launch a dedicated product line for the wellness segment. If done well, closing half the share gap — recovering roughly 3.5 percentage points from 24% to about 27.5% — would represent approximately $280M in incremental revenue on the $8B women's base.

title: Hilton Loyalty Program Expansion
prompt: You are a VP of marketing at Hilton. Hilton Honors has 173 million members but only 38% are active (stayed at least once in the past 12 months). The loyalty program drives 60% of Hilton's occupancy. How would you grow revenue from the existing loyalty base?
context: Marriott Bonvoy has 196 million members with 45% active rate. Hilton's average member stay is 2.1 nights per trip vs. 2.6 for Marriott. Members who use the Hilton app book direct 3x more often than non-app users. Hilton has 7,200 properties across 123 countries. Non-hotel partnership redemptions (airlines, retail) represent only 8% of points usage vs. 22% for competitors.
whyThisFramework: 3C's Growth Framework to identify which customer segments are most valuable and underserved, then 4P's to design engagement tactics (product: app features, price: point value, place: partnership channels, promotion: re-engagement campaigns) that drive active stays.
keyConsiderations: What causes 62% of members to go inactive — price, preference, or awareness?
Which member segments have highest lifetime value and lowest activation?
Can non-hotel partnerships convert passive members into active travelers?
How does Hilton compete with Airbnb for the millennial leisure traveler?
clarifyingQuestions: When we say 38% active, is that defined as at least one stay in the past 12 months — and how does activeness correlate with revenue per member versus the inactive 62%?
Do we know why members go inactive — is it primarily switching to competitors like Marriott, choosing Airbnb for leisure, or simply not traveling enough to stay at hotels?
What is the current split of active members by segment — business travelers, leisure travelers, corporate account — and which segment has the worst activation rate?
For the app engagement data showing 3x direct booking rate, how large is the current app user base and what's the main barrier to app adoption among non-users?

subIssues: Which inactive member segments have the highest travel frequency but are staying at competitors — and can targeted offers win them back?
What is the minimum investment (offer, communication, experience) required to convert an inactive member to one stay per year?
Are partnership redemptions (airlines, retail) a credible bridge to re-engage members who aren't actively traveling for hotel stays?

meceBlocks.block: Increase Nights per Stay for Active Members
subIssues: Why does Hilton average 2.1 nights vs. Marriott's 2.6 — is it property mix, business vs. leisure split, or booking incentives?
What incentives would cause an active member to extend a stay by even 0.5 nights on average — what's the revenue and margin impact?
Can app-based personalization (local recommendations, upgrade offers) be used to drive longer stays among existing bookers?

meceBlocks.block: Drive Direct Booking via App Adoption
subIssues: Given app users book direct 3x more, what is the ROI of a campaign to triple app downloads among the existing inactive member base?
What features — mobile check-in, room selection, local experiences — would drive app adoption for members who currently book through OTAs?
Can Hilton partner with airlines or corporate travel platforms to make the Hilton app a default booking tool for business travelers?

stepByStep.title: Quantify the revenue opportunity
stepByStep.detail: Establish the math: Hilton Honors drives 60% of occupancy; if 62% of 173 million members are inactive, that's roughly 107 million dormant members. Even converting 5% of inactive members to one stay per year at an average of $150/night for 2 nights = approximately $1.6 billion in incremental revenue (5% × 107M = 5.35M members; 5.35M × $150 × 2 nights = $1.605B). Even modest conversion rates represent a massive opportunity relative to any reactivation investment.

stepByStep.title: Segment the inactive base
stepByStep.detail: Request segmentation of the 107 million inactive members by travel frequency (high, medium, low travelers), last-stay date, and competitor usage data where available. Prioritize the 'high-frequency traveler who has lapsed' segment — they travel but are choosing competitors — as the highest ROI reactivation target.

stepByStep.title: Design reactivation and retention tactics via 4P's
stepByStep.detail: Product: Enhance the Hilton app with room selection, digital key, and local experiences to close the app adoption gap. Price: Offer lapsed members a 'Welcome Back' bonus points campaign (double points for first two stays) rather than discounting rate. Place: Expand non-hotel partnerships from 8% to 15%+ of redemption options — airline miles, dining, retail — to keep Hilton Honors top-of-mind for members not actively traveling. Promotion: Launch a personalized reactivation email sequence with dynamic offers based on member travel history.

stepByStep.title: Close the nights-per-stay gap vs. Marriott
stepByStep.detail: The 2.1 vs. 2.6 nights gap represents a 24% differential. Investigate whether this is a property mix issue (more city hotels vs. resort/conference properties) or a booking incentive issue. Recommend in-stay upgrade offers and points accelerators for extending stays by one night, and prioritize positioning properties with conference and extended-stay appeal to corporate accounts.

stepByStep.title: Recommend and prioritize initiatives
stepByStep.detail: Prioritize: (1) App adoption campaign for high-frequency lapsed members — highest near-term revenue impact given the 3x booking multiplier. (2) Partnership expansion — keeps brand relevant for non-travel spend. (3) In-stay extension incentives — relatively low-cost and directly addresses the Marriott nights gap. Avoid deep rate discounting, which would erode margin without building loyalty.

keyTerms.definition: The percentage of loyalty program members who made at least one qualifying transaction (here, one stay) in the past 12 months — Hilton's 38% vs. Marriott's 45% represents a significant engagement gap
A reservation made through Hilton's own website or app rather than an OTA (Online Travel Agency) like Expedia — direct bookings have higher margins because Hilton avoids the 15-25% OTA commission
The fee (typically 15-25% of room revenue) paid to Online Travel Agencies like Expedia or Booking.com when a customer books through their platform instead of directly with Hilton
The financial obligation Hilton carries for unredeemed loyalty points outstanding on member accounts — expanding non-hotel redemptions reduces this liability while maintaining member engagement
The percentage of inactive loyalty members who make at least one qualifying stay within a defined period after a targeted campaign — the key metric for evaluating re-engagement program ROI

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer opens with a revenue quantification of the inactive member opportunity, uses the 4P's to generate structured tactical options, and makes a clear prioritization call (app adoption and reactivation over discounting). A weak answer lists generic loyalty program tactics without connecting them to the specific data points provided — the 38% active rate, 2.1 night gap, 3x app booking rate, and 8% non-hotel redemption rate are all flags that need to be addressed.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Great question. We're really solving two problems here: reactivating the 62% of Honors members who aren't generating any revenue, and deepening engagement with the 38% who are active to increase their value. I'd like to structure my response using the 3C's to diagnose the opportunity, then 4P's to build the tactical plan. May I ask a couple of questions first?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: First, do we know why members go inactive — is it primarily competitive switching to Marriott Bonvoy, or is it a broader 'not traveling' issue? And second, is there a revenue breakdown by active vs. inactive members — I want to understand the upside of moving even a fraction of that inactive base. Thank you — I'll proceed with those assumptions.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Quantifying the Inactive Opportunity
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with the math. We have 173 million Honors members, 38% active — so roughly 107 million inactive members. If we could convert just 5% of them to one stay per year at an average of 2 nights at $150 per night, let me work through that: 5% of 107 million is about 5.35 million members. Multiply by 2 nights at $150 — that's roughly $1.6 billion in incremental revenue. That's a substantial opportunity even if our actual conversion rate is a fraction of that 5% target. The question is: which lever has the highest ROI?

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — 4P's Tactical Framework
sampleResponse.script: Let me walk through 4P's to build the plan. On Product: the app engagement data is the most actionable signal we have — members who use the Hilton app book direct 3 times more often than non-users. That means our highest-ROI product investment is driving app adoption among lapsed members — enhanced features like digital key, room selection, and local experience recommendations. On Price: I would not recommend discounting room rates — that erodes margin and trains members to expect discounts. Instead, I'd run a 'Welcome Back' double-points promotion for the first two stays, which feels like value without pricing down. On Place: the non-hotel partnerships are a glaring gap — 8% of points redeemed through airlines and retail versus 22% for competitors. Expanding those partnerships keeps Hilton Honors top-of-mind for members between trips, which is critical for reactivation. On Promotion: a personalized reactivation sequence for inactive members — segmented by travel history — offering targeted bonus points or upgrade guarantees for the next booking.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: I'd prioritize three initiatives in this order. First, an app adoption campaign targeting high-frequency lapsed members — this directly leverages the 3x direct-booking multiplier and builds a long-term engagement habit. Second, expand non-hotel redemption partnerships from 8% toward the competitive benchmark of 22% — this keeps the brand relevant between trips and reduces points liability. Third, address the 2.1 vs. 2.6 nights gap through in-stay incentives — upgrade offers and points accelerators for extended stays. I'd explicitly avoid broad rate discounting. The inactive member base is a substantial untapped asset — even moving the active rate from 38% to 42% represents a meaningful revenue gain, and we have the data and tools to make that happen without discounting our way there.

title: Kraft Mac & Cheese Premiumization
prompt: You are a brand director at Kraft Heinz. Kraft Mac & Cheese generates $1.2B in annual US retail sales but volume has declined 3% year-over-year for two consecutive years while the premium pasta category has grown 9%. How do you grow Kraft Mac & Cheese revenue?
context: The premium mac & cheese segment (Annie's, Banza, local brands) grew from $180M to $290M over 3 years. Kraft's household penetration is 43% — one of the highest in the category — but purchase frequency has dropped from 6.2 to 5.7 times per year. Millennial parents are the fastest-declining segment, citing concerns about artificial ingredients. Kraft reformulated to remove artificial preservatives in 2016 but awareness of the change is only 31% among millennial parents.
whyThisFramework: STP to identify which segments are lapsing vs. growing and whether a sub-brand or repositioning can capture premium-leaning millennials without alienating core buyers, then 4P's to redesign product formulation communication, pricing architecture, and shelf placement strategy.
keyConsiderations: Should Kraft launch a premium sub-brand or reposition the core brand?
How does Kraft communicate the 2016 reformulation to skeptical millennial parents?
What price premium can Kraft command before losing its value-positioning advantage?
Is the decline a product problem or a perception problem?
clarifyingQuestions: The 3% volume decline — is it driven by fewer households buying (penetration falling from 43%) or the same households buying less frequently (purchase frequency down to 5.7), and which is larger?
Is the millennial parent attrition primarily going to Annie's and Banza, or to home-made pasta alternatives — knowing where defectors go matters for our response strategy?
What is Kraft's current price per serving relative to Annie's and Banza — are we talking a 30% premium gap or a 100% gap?
Do we have any data on what would change millennial parents' minds — would learning about the 2016 reformulation be sufficient, or is the brand itself the problem?

subIssues: Is the millennial decline caused by Kraft's actual ingredient profile or by outdated perceptions — given 69% of millennial parents don't know about the 2016 reformulation?
If perception is the primary problem, what message and channel would most efficiently close the awareness gap from 31% to a tipping-point level?

Are there any remaining product attributes (sodium, artificial flavors beyond preservatives) that would still disqualify Kraft for health-conscious millennial parents even after full reformulation awareness?
Can the core Kraft Mac & Cheese brand be repositioned for millennials without alienating the value-focused core buyer who represents the majority of the 43% penetration?
Would a premium sub-brand (e.g., 'Kraft Organics' or 'Kraft Simply') capture millennial parents who are driving Annie's growth without brand confusion?
What are the cannibalization risks and incremental revenue estimates of each architecture approach?
The purchase frequency dropped from 6.2 to 5.7 times per year — what occasions or meal contexts have been lost and can they be recaptured through product or marketing?
Are there new product formats (cups, frozen, premium sauce pouches) that could add purchase occasions without diluting the core SKU?
How does Kraft's price point relative to homemade pasta influence frequency — is value vs. convenience the real decision driver?

stepByStep.title: Diagnose the volume loss
stepByStep.detail: Calculate the revenue impact of the frequency decline: 43% household penetration on ~130 million US households is roughly 56 million buying households. A drop from 6.2 to 5.7 purchases per year is 0.5 fewer purchases × 56 million households = 28 million lost unit occasions. At an average unit price of ~$1.80, that's roughly $50M in annual revenue erosion — purely from frequency, before any penetration decline.

stepByStep.title: Identify which segments are driving the decline
stepByStep.detail: Apply STP to segment the buyer base into: (A) value-focused loyal buyers (older households, price-sensitive), (B) millennial parents declining due to ingredient perception, and (C) lapsed buyers who moved to premium alternatives. Millennial parents are the most actionable segment — they're leaving over a perception problem that the 2016 reformulation has already solved.

stepByStep.title: Decide: reposition core brand or launch sub-brand
stepByStep.detail: Evaluate two options. Option A: reposition Kraft Mac & Cheese with a 'clean label' campaign — 'No artificial preservatives since 2016' messaging prominently on packaging, in digital and in-store. Risk: alienates value buyers if too premium. Option B: launch a premium sub-brand (e.g., Kraft Organics Mac & Cheese) to compete directly with Annie's at a higher price point. Risk: fragmented investment, lower scale. Recommend Option A first — it has zero incremental COGS and can be tested with a packaging update and campaign before any sub-brand investment.

stepByStep.title: Build the 4P's action plan
stepByStep.detail: Product: prominently display 'No artificial preservatives' and relevant certifications on packaging front panel — awareness is only 31%. Price: test a modest 10-15% price increase on premium SKUs (shapes, characters) while maintaining the entry price point. Place: negotiate additional shelf space in natural/organic aisles at retailers like Target and Whole Foods alongside conventional shelf. Promotion: run a millennial-targeted digital campaign 'The Kraft You Already Love, Made Cleaner' — targeted at parents on Instagram and YouTube.

stepByStep.title: Set metrics and success criteria
stepByStep.detail: Define success as: (1) increase millennial parent awareness of the 2016 reformulation from 31% to 50% within 12 months via campaign tracking. (2) Stabilize purchase frequency from 5.7 back toward 5.9 within 18 months. (3) Grow premium SKUs (shapes, cups, flavored varieties) to 20% of Kraft Mac & Cheese revenue, up from current level.

keyTerms.definition: The percentage of all US households that purchased Kraft Mac & Cheese at least once in the past year — Kraft's 43% is exceptionally high, meaning the problem is frequency, not reach
How often the average buying household purchases the product per year — Kraft's drop from 6.2 to 5.7 is an 8% decline in buying rate among existing customers
A product positioning strategy emphasizing the removal of artificial ingredients, additives, and preservatives — the core driver of premium mac & cheese growth via Annie's and Banza
How Kraft organizes its products — whether to stretch the core Kraft brand upmarket or create a distinct premium sub-brand to avoid confusing value shoppers

How much purchase volume changes in response to a price change (either up or down) — Kraft must estimate whether a 10-15% price bump on premium SKUs would be offset by margin gains or cause volume loss

interviewerExpectations: An impressive answer uses the frequency math to quantify the problem precisely, makes a clear and reasoned choice between repositioning the core brand vs. launching a sub-brand, and calls out the 2016 reformulation as the highest-ROI intervention — it's already done and just needs communication. A weak answer treats this as a generic 'declining brand' problem without engaging with the specific data: the 31% awareness stat, the 6.2-to-5.7 frequency drop, and the $180M-to-$290M premium segment growth.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Thank you. This is a brand in decline that has both a perception problem and a frequency problem — and they're related. I want to use STP to identify which consumer segments we should prioritize, then 4P's to build the plan. Before I do, can I ask a quick question?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Is the 3% volume decline driven primarily by purchase frequency falling — which the data suggests at 6.2 to 5.7 times per year — or are we also losing household penetration below 43%? And is the millennial parent attrition primarily going to Annie's, or to home-cooked alternatives? Great — that confirms the frequency issue is the primary driver and Annie's is the main competitor for millennial parents.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Quantifying the Problem
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with the math. Kraft has roughly 43% of 130 million US households buying — that's about 56 million buying households. The frequency drop of 0.5 purchases per year across those households is about 28 million lost purchase occasions annually. At roughly $1.80 per unit, that's around $50 million in lost revenue per year purely from frequency decline, and it's accelerating. The premium segment growing from $180M to $290M tells us those occasions aren't disappearing — they're migrating to Annie's and Banza.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — STP Diagnosis and 4P's Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: Segmenting Kraft's buyers: we have a value-loyal core that's price-sensitive and brand-habitual — we should not risk alienating them. And we have a millennial parent segment that's lapsing over ingredient concerns — but here's the key insight: the 2016 reformulation already solved the actual problem. Kraft removed artificial preservatives nine years ago, but only 31% of millennial parents know that. That means the single highest-ROI action we can take is a communication campaign, not a product change. On Product: update the front-of-pack to prominently feature 'No artificial preservatives.' On Promotion: run a targeted digital campaign for millennial parents — something like 'Kraft Mac & Cheese: made cleaner since 2016' — on YouTube and Instagram. On Price: I'd test a modest 10% increase on premium SKUs like character shapes and flavored varieties to capture incremental margin while keeping the flagship price stable. On Place: explore whether Kraft can earn shelf space in the natural food aisles at Target and Whole Foods alongside the conventional aisle.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: In summary, this is primarily a perception problem, not a product problem. The most urgent action is closing the 31% awareness gap on the 2016 reformulation — this is low-cost and high-impact. I would not recommend launching a premium sub-brand as a first move because it fragments marketing investment and risks confusing our core buyer. Instead: reposition the core brand as 'cleaner than you think,' stabilize frequency among millennial parents, and test premium pricing on specialty SKUs. If we can recover frequency from 5.7 to 6.0 purchases per year across 56 million buying households at the current price point, that's roughly $30M in recovered revenue annually — calculated as 0.3 additional purchases times 56 million households times $1.80 per unit. It's not a massive number, but it's the floor; the bigger prize is stopping the migration to Annie's and recapturing the millennial parent segment for the long term.

title: Amex Small Business Card Growth
prompt: You are a director of product marketing at American Express. The Amex Business Gold Card has a 14% market share among small business credit cards but only 6% among businesses with fewer than 10 employees. Small businesses under 10 employees represent 78% of all US small businesses. How do you grow penetration in this segment?
context: Chase Ink and Capital One Spark collectively hold 51% of the under-10-employee segment. These competitors offer lower annual fees ($95 vs. Amex Business Gold's $295) and simpler rewards structures. Survey data shows 67% of micro-business owners cite annual fee as primary barrier to Amex adoption. Amex's NPS among existing small business cardholders is +52, among the highest in the category. Referral rates from existing members are 3x higher than industry average.
whyThisFramework: 3C's to identify the micro-business segment's unmet needs vs. what competitors offer vs. Amex's actual strengths (high NPS, referral network), then 4P's to address the price barrier (fee structure), product fit (simplified rewards), and promotion (referral program amplification).
keyConsiderations: Is fee sensitivity the real barrier or is it awareness and perceived value?
Should Amex launch a new lower-fee product or restructure Business Gold?
How can Amex leverage its +52 NPS to drive referral-based growth?
What rewards categories matter most to micro-business owners?
clarifyingQuestions: Of the 67% who cite annual fee as a barrier, do we know whether they'd convert at $150 or $195 — is this true price sensitivity or a 'default excuse' masking a deeper perception gap about Amex's value?
What is the spend profile of a typical micro-business owner with fewer than 10 employees — do they spend enough monthly to make the $295 fee economics work on 4x rewards points?
When you say referral rates are 3x industry average, are those referrals converting to new cardholders — and are the referred customers staying active?
Are we constrained to growing the Business Gold Card specifically, or is a new lower-fee product an option within scope?

subIssues: At what monthly spend level does the Business Gold Card's rewards value exceed the $295 annual fee relative to Chase Ink at $95 — is there a clear break-even spend that most micro-businesses don't reach?
Would introducing a lower-fee Business Card at $150 capture the fee-sensitive segment, or would it cannibalize Business Gold revenue from the existing 14% share base?
Can Amex demonstrate ROI through a 'rewards calculator' tool that shows micro-business owners their net savings based on spending categories?
Are micro-business owners declining Amex because of the fee alone, or because they find the 4x rewards categories complex relative to Chase Ink's flat cash-back structure?
What spending categories do micro-businesses under 10 employees over-index on — software subscriptions, shipping, advertising — and does Business Gold's 4x cover those?
Would a simpler rewards structure (flat 2% cash back) outperform the current tiered system for micro-business acquisition, even if it's less differentiated at the high end?
With a +52 NPS and 3x referral rate, what is the current referral program structure and why isn't it already driving micro-business penetration?
Can Amex build a formal peer referral network among micro-business owners — targeting accountants, SCORE advisors, and small business networks as referral channels?
What is the lifetime value of a referred micro-business cardholder vs. one acquired through paid channels, and does the math support a higher referral bonus investment?

stepByStep.title: Quantify the market opportunity
stepByStep.detail: Establish the size of the prize: 78% of US small businesses have fewer than 10 employees. We should clarify the total micro-business cardholder universe and Amex's current 6% penetration within it as our starting point. At 6% penetration vs. Amex's 14% overall small-business share, closing even half the gap would imply millions of potential new cardholders. To size revenue potential precisely, ask for the current micro-business cardholder count and average annual spend — that gives us the dollar value of each penetration point. Note: any market size estimate for total segment spend volume should be confirmed with Amex's internal data rather than assumed.

stepByStep.title: Test whether fee is the real barrier
stepByStep.detail: Conduct a value ROI analysis: Business Gold offers 4x points on top two eligible spending categories. If a micro-business spends $3,000/month on ads and software (common for a 5-person firm), the 4x points at ~$0.01-per-point value = $1,440/year in rewards, well above the $295 fee. Request data on whether micro-business owners who were shown this math in research changed their stated intent. This distinguishes true fee sensitivity from an awareness-of-value problem.

stepByStep.title: Decide on product architecture
stepByStep.detail: Evaluate two paths: (A) Launch a new Amex Business Essential card at $150/year with simplified flat 2x rewards — targets price-sensitive micro-businesses and blocks Chase Ink. (B) Add a first-year fee waiver to Business Gold with a rewards calculator marketing campaign — converts aware-but-hesitant micro-businesses without a new product. Recommend Option A if research confirms true price elasticity, Option B if it confirms awareness gap.

stepByStep.title: Amplify the referral engine
stepByStep.detail: The 3x referral rate is the most underleveraged asset. Design a 'Small Business Refer' program: existing Business Gold cardholders who refer a micro-business owner get 20,000 bonus points (worth ~$200) and the new member gets a first-year fee waiver. Target accountants, SCORE mentors, and small business association networks as high-leverage referral channels — one accountant with 50 micro-business clients can be a multiplier.

stepByStep.title: Set 90-day and 12-month metrics
stepByStep.detail: 90-day: launch referral program amplification and rewards ROI calculator campaign — measure card applications from micro-businesses against baseline. 12-month goal: grow micro-business penetration from 6% to 9% (a 50% relative gain) and measure approval-to-activation rate and 6-month spend to ensure quality of new members.

keyTerms.definition: A measure of customer loyalty on a -100 to +100 scale based on how likely customers are to recommend a product — Amex's +52 is exceptionally high and signals untapped referral potential

The calculation of whether a credit card's rewards and benefits exceed its annual fee for a given spending profile — the primary mental hurdle for micro-business owners considering the $295 Business Gold
Businesses with fewer than 10 employees — representing 78% of all US small businesses and the highest-growth opportunity for business card acquisition
Waiving the first-year annual fee as an acquisition incentive — reduces the upfront barrier without permanently repricing the card

A growth loop where satisfied customers bring in new customers through referrals, compounding acquisition without proportional marketing spend — Amex's 3x referral rate represents an underinvested version of this

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer diagnoses whether the $295 fee barrier is real or perceived (do the rewards math out?), makes a specific product architecture recommendation (new tier vs. waiver campaign), and proposes a concrete referral program design using the +52 NPS as an asset. A weak answer accepts the 67% fee-barrier survey result at face value without testing whether a spend-based ROI analysis changes the picture, or proposes simply lowering the fee without considering cannibalization.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: This is a penetration problem with a specific segment — micro-businesses under 10 employees — and I want to be careful not to assume the 67% fee-barrier survey answer is the complete story. I'll use 3C's to diagnose the real barrier first, then 4P's to build the response. A quick clarifying question before I dive in.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: When 67% of micro-business owners cite the annual fee as a barrier, do we have any data on whether showing them the rewards ROI — what they'd actually earn back based on their typical spending — changes their stated intent to apply? And separately, are we open to launching a new lower-fee product, or is the goal to grow Business Gold specifically? Perfect — knowing both will shape the recommendation significantly.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — 3C's Diagnosis
sampleResponse.script: Starting with the Consumer: the micro-business owner with fewer than 10 employees is typically spending heavily on software subscriptions, advertising, and maybe shipping — the exact categories Business Gold's 4x multiplier covers. But they're likely not doing the math. A business spending $3,000 a month in those categories earns roughly $1,440 in points annually at a $0.01 per point value — which more than covers the $295 fee. So the fee may be a perception problem, not a true price problem. On Competition: Chase Ink and Capital One Spark hold 51% of this segment with $95 fees and flat cash-back structures. They win on simplicity and apparent affordability, not on reward value. On the Company side: Amex has a +52 NPS and a 3x referral rate — both are underinvested assets in driving micro-business acquisition.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — 4P's Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: On Product: I recommend launching a new Amex Business Essential tier at $150 per year with a simplified 2x rewards structure — this directly addresses the fee barrier and blocks Chase Ink, while protecting Business Gold for higher-spend businesses. On Price: for Business Gold, run a first-year fee waiver offer paired with a personalized rewards calculator that shows the prospect their estimated annual rewards based on their industry's typical spend profile. On Promotion: this is where the +52 NPS becomes a weapon — launch a 'Refer a Business Owner' program with a 20,000-point bonus for referrers and a first-year fee waiver for referred new members. Target accountants and SCORE advisors specifically — one accountant with 50 micro-business clients can drive dozens of applications. On Place: partner with QuickBooks, FreshBooks, and small business banking apps to surface the Amex Business Essential offer at the moment micro-business owners are managing their finances — that's the highest-intent moment.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: To summarize: I believe this is partly a price problem and largely a value-communication problem. My recommendation is two parallel moves. First, launch an Amex Business Essential card at $150 to capture the truly fee-sensitive micro-business segment — this addresses the 67% barrier directly. Second, invest in making the referral flywheel work at scale — with a +52 NPS and 3x referral rate, we have the raw material for a growth engine that doesn't require expensive paid acquisition. To size the prize: I'd ask Amex's internal data team for the current micro-business cardholder count and average annual spend, and calculate the revenue value of each penetration point from there — rather than assume a market size. If we can move micro-business penetration from 6% to 9% over 18 months, and these businesses average $50,000 in annual card spend at a 2% net revenue yield, each additional percentage point of penetration is worth roughly $30M in net revenue at current segment size — a very compelling ROI for the referral and product investments required.

title: Dick's Private Label Expansion
prompt: You are VP of merchandising strategy at Dick's Sporting Goods. Dick's private label brands (CALIA, Alpine Design, DBX) represent 14% of total revenue at $1.9B. National brand equivalents like Under Armour and The North Face carry 40-55% gross margins vs. private label's 58-62%. How do you grow private label to 20% of revenue over 3 years?
context: Dick's operates 850 stores and $12.4B in annual revenue. Target's private label Cat & Jack reached $2B in revenue within 2 years of launch. Nike has threatened to reduce shelf space allocation at Dick's over private label expansion. Private label conversion rates are highest in fitness equipment (23%) and lowest in footwear (4%). Consumer research shows 71% of Dick's shoppers are unaware that CALIA is a Dick's brand.
whyThisFramework: Value Chain Analysis to identify where Dick's has cost and differentiation advantages in private label (design, manufacturing partnerships, merchandising) vs. national brands, then 4P's to build a go-to-market plan including product category prioritization, pricing architecture, placement strategy, and brand-building investment.
keyConsiderations: Which categories have the best private label conversion potential beyond fitness equipment?
How does Dick's manage the Nike relationship risk while expanding private label?
What investment in brand awareness is needed to make CALIA a recognized brand?
Should Dick's acquire a brand vs. build in-house?
clarifyingQuestions: The 14% private label share at $1.9B — is that primarily CALIA, or are Alpine Design and DBX material contributors, and which brand has the highest customer satisfaction scores?
When Nike threatens to reduce shelf space, do we have a sense of what the revenue impact would be if Nike pulled 20% of its allocation — is this a real risk or a negotiating posture?
For the categories between fitness equipment's 23% conversion and footwear's 4%, do we have conversion data for apparel, accessories, and outdoor gear specifically?
